title: EXPOSÉ XIII THE FINITENESS THEOREMS FOR THE PICARD FUNCTOR

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XIII THE FINITENESS THEOREMS FOR THE PICARD FUNCTOR

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XIII
THE FINITENESS THEOREMS FOR THE PICARD FUNCTORby S. Kleiman
1. \(b\)-sheaves
Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(X\) a projective \(k\)-scheme, equipped with an ample invertible module \(\mathcal O_X(1)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1 (Mumford).

Let \(m\in\mathbb Z\). A coherent sheaf \(F\) on \(X\) is said to be \(m\)-regular if, at each point of the support of \(F\), \(\mathcal O_X(1)\) is generated by its global sections and if, for every \(q\geq1\), we have

\[H^q(F(m-q))=0\qquad.\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.

Let \(0\to F(-1)\to F\to G\to0\) be an exact sequence of coherent sheaves on \(X\) (*). If \(F\) is \(m\)-regular, then so is \(G\).
Indeed, we have the corresponding exact sequence:
\[H^q(F(m-q))\longrightarrow H^q(G(m-q))\longrightarrow H^{q+1}(F(m-q-1))\qquad.\]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3.

If a sheaf \(F\) on \(X\) is \(m\)-regular, then, for every \(n\geq m\), we have:

(i) \(F\) is \(n\)-regular.

(ii) \(H^0(F(n))\otimes H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\to H^0(F(n+1))\) is surjective.

(iii) \(F(n)\) is generated by \(H^0(F(n))\).

The proof proceeds by induction on the dimension \(s\) of the support of \(F\); when \(s=-1\), it is trivial. Take a section \(\sigma\in H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\) which vanishes at no point of the finite set \(\operatorname{Ass}(F)\); then \(\sigma\) defines an exact sequence \(0\to F(-1)\to F\to G\to0\), and \(G\) is \(m\)-regular by 1.2, with support of dimension \(s-1\).
Consider the corresponding exact sequence:
\[H^q(F(n-q-1))\longrightarrow H^q(F(n-q))\longrightarrow H^q(G(n-q)).\]
By the induction hypothesis, we have \(H^q(G(n-q))=0\) for \(n\geq m\), so \(H^q(F(n-q-1))=0\) implies \(H^q(F(n-q))=0\); but \(H^q(F(m-q))=0\), which gives (i).
To establish (ii), we chase through the following commutative diagram:
\[\xymatrix{ &H^0(F(n))\otimes H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\ar[r]\ar[d]&H^0(G(n))\otimes H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]&H^0(F(n))\ar[r]\ar[ur]&H^0(F(n+1))\ar[r]&H^0(G(n+1))\ar[d]\\ &&&0 }\]

where the oblique arrow is defined by \(\varphi\mapsto\varphi\otimes\sigma\), the horizontal rows are exact, the first by the \(n\)-regularity of \(F\) and by (i), already proved, and the second column is exact by the induction hypothesis.
Finally, (iii) follows from the following commutative diagram:
\[\xymatrix{ H^0(F(n))\otimes H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))_X\ar[r]^{\alpha_n}\ar[d]&H^0(F(n)(1))_X\ar[d]^{\beta_{n+1}}\\ [H^0(F(n))_X](1)\ar[r]^{\beta_n\otimes\mathrm{id}}&F(n)(1) }\]

where, if \(M\) is a vector space over \(k\), we write \(M_X\) for \(M\otimes_k\mathcal O_X\). For \(n\geq m\), \(\alpha_n\) is surjective by (ii); therefore, if \(\beta_{n+1}\) is surjective, then so is \(\beta_n\). By Serre's theorem, \(\beta_{n+1}\) is surjective for \(n\gg0\); whence (iii).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4.

Let \(0\to F(-1)\to F\to G\to0\) be an exact sequence on \(X\). If \(G\) is \(m\)-regular, then we have:

(i) \(H^q(F(n))=0\) for \(q\geq2\) and \(n\geq m-q\).

(ii) \(h^1(F(n-1))\geq h^1(F(n))\) for \(n\geq m-1\).

(iii) \(H^1(F(n))=0\) for \(n\geq(m-1)+h^1(F(m-1))\).

In particular, \(F\) is \([m+h^1(F(m-1))]\)-regular.
Indeed, in the exact sequence
\[H^{q-1}(G(n))\longrightarrow H^q(F(n-1))\longrightarrow H^q(F(n))\longrightarrow H^q(G(n))\qquad,\]

the two extreme groups are 0 for \(q\geq2\) and \(n\geq m-(q-1)\) by 2.3 (i). Therefore \(H^q(F(m-q))\widetilde\longrightarrow H^q(F(m-q+1))\widetilde\longrightarrow H^q(F(m-q+2))\widetilde\longrightarrow\cdots\), but, by Serre's theorem, \(H^q(F(n))=0\) for \(n\gg0\); whence (i).
For \(n\geq m-1\), consider the following exact sequence:
\[0\to H^0(F(n-1))\to H^0(F(n))\xrightarrow{\alpha_n}H^0(G(n))\to H^1(F(n-1))\to H^1(F(n))\to0.\]
It implies (ii). Now, suppose \(\alpha_n\) is surjective and consider the following commutative diagram:

\[\xymatrix{ H^0(F(n))\otimes H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\ar[r]^{\alpha_n\otimes\mathrm{id}}\ar[d]&H^0(G(n))\otimes H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\ar[r]\ar[d]^{\beta_n}&0\\ H^0(F(n+1))\ar[r]^{\alpha_{n+1}}&H^0(G(n+1)) }\]

If \(n\geq m\), then \(\beta_n\) is surjective by 2.3 (ii); hence \(\alpha_{n+1}\) is surjective. Consequently, \(H^1(F(n-1))\twoheadrightarrow H^1(F(n))\twoheadrightarrow\cdots\twoheadrightarrow0\). On the other hand, if \(\alpha_n\) is not surjective, then \(h^1(F(n-1))>h^1(F(n))\); therefore \(n\mapsto h^1(F(n))\) is strictly decreasing from \(h^1(F(m-1))\) on, until it becomes 0; whence (iii).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.5.

Let \(F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\), \(r\) an integer \(\geq\dim\operatorname{Supp}(F)\) and \((b)=(b_0,\dots,b_r)\) a sequence of \(r+1\) integers. Then \(F\) is said to be a \((b)\)-sheaf if it satisfies one of the two following equivalent sets of conditions:

(1.5.1) (i) At each point of the support of \(F\), \(\mathcal O_X(1)\) is generated by \(H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\).

(ii) \(h^0(F(-1))\leq b_0\).

(iii) (When \(r\geq1\)), there exists a section \(\sigma\in H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\) which defines an exact sequence \(0\to F(-1)\to F\to G\to0\) such that \(G\) is a \((b_1,\dots,b_r)\)-sheaf.

(1.5.2) There exists a \(F\)-regular sequence of \(r\) sections \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_r\in H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\) such that, for \(i=0,\dots,r\), if \(F_i\) denotes the restriction of \(F\) to the scheme of zeros of \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_i\), then \(h^0(F_i(-1))\leq b_i\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.6.

Let \(F\) be a \((b)\)-sheaf on \(X\). Then:

(i) For every "sufficiently general" sequence \(\sigma\) of \(r\) sections \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_r\in H^0(\mathcal O_X(1))\), if \(F_{\sigma,i}\) denotes the restriction of \(F\) to the scheme of zeros of \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_i\), then \(h^0(F_{\sigma,i}(-1))\leq b_i\), where \(0\leq i\leq r\).

(ii) Every subsheaf \(G\) of \(F\) is a \((b)\)-sheaf.

Indeed, let \(S'\) be the affine space \(\mathbb A_k^N\) whose \(k\)-points correspond to the sequences \(\sigma\), and \(T\) the open subscheme of \(S\) corresponding to the \(F\)-regular sequences. For fixed \(i\), the sheaves \(F_{\sigma,i}(-1)\) which correspond to the \(k\)-points of \(T\) are contained in a family flat over \(T\), and by hypothesis \(T\) is non-empty. Therefore (i) follows from the upper semi-continuity property of the function \(\sigma\longmapsto h^0(F_{\sigma,i}(-1))\) (EGA III, 7.7.5). Finally, since every "sufficiently general" sequence is \(G\)-regular and such that \(G_{\sigma,i}\) is a subsheaf of \(F_{\sigma,i}\) for every \(i\), (ii) follows from (i).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.7.

Let \(0\to F(-1)\to F\to G\to0\) be an exact sequence on \(X\). If the Hilbert polynomial of \(F\) is written in the form

\[\chi(F(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i\binom{n+i}{i},\]

then

\[\chi(G(n))=\sum_{i=0}^{r-1}a_{i+1}\binom{n+i}{i}\qquad.\]
Indeed, \(\chi(G(n))=\chi(F(n))-\chi(F(n-1))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i[\binom{n+i}{i}-\binom{n-1+i}{i}]\); therefore \(\chi(G(n))=\sum_{i=1}^r a_i\binom{n+i-1}{i-1}\), whence the assertion.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.8.

Let \(F\) be a \((b)\)-sheaf on \(X\), \(s=\dim\operatorname{Supp}(F)\) and \(\chi(F(n))=\sum_{i=0}^s a_i\binom{n+i}{i}\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(F\). Then:

(i) For \(n\geq-1\), \(h^0(F(n))\leq\sum_{i=0}^s b_i\binom{n+i}{i}\).

(ii) \(a_s\leq b_s\) and \(F\) is also a \((b_0,\dots,b_{s-1},a_s)\)-sheaf.
Indeed, let us argue by induction on \(s\). When \(s=0\), it is clear since \(a_0=h^0(F)=h^0(F(-1))\leq b_0\). When \(s\geq1\), we have an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow F(-1)\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow0\]
with \(G\) a \((b_1,\dots,b_r)\)-sheaf and \(\dim\operatorname{Supp}(G)=s-1\). Then
\[h^0(F(n))-h^0(F(n-1))\leq h^0(G(n)),\]
but
\[h^0(G(n))\leq\sum_{i=0}^{s-1}b_{i+1}\binom{n+i}{i}\]

by induction on \(s\) and \(h^0(F(-1))\leq b_0\); whence (i) by induction on \(n\geq-1\). Finally, by 1.7 and induction on \(s\), \(a_s\leq b_s\) and \(G\) is a \((b_1,\dots,b_{s-1},a_s)\)-sheaf; whence (ii).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.9.

We call \((b)\)-polynomials the following polynomials with rational coefficients \(\geq0\), defined by induction:

\[\left\{\begin{aligned} P_{-1}&=0\\ P_r(X_0,\dots,X_r)&=P_{r-1}(X_1,\dots,X_r)+\sum_{i=0}^r X_i\binom{P_{r-1}(X_1,\dots,X_r)-1+i}{i}. \end{aligned}\right.\]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.10.

For the \((b)\)-polynomials, one sees at once by induction on \(r\geq t\) that

\[P_r(X_0,\dots,X_t,0,\dots,0)=P_t(X_0,\dots,X_t)\qquad.\]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.11. (The main theorem on \((b)\)-sheaves).

Let \(F\) be a \((b)\)-sheaf on \(X\) and \(\chi(F(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i\binom{n+i}{i}\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(F\); let \((c)=(c_0,\dots,c_r)\) be a sequence of integers \(\geq(b)-(a)\) and \(m=P_r(c)\) the integer given by the \(r\)-th \((b)\)-polynomial (1.9). Then \(m\geq0\) and \(F\) is \(m\)-regular. In particular, if \(s=\dim\operatorname{Supp}(F)\), then \(F\) is \(P_{s-1}(c_0,\dots,c_{s-1})\)-regular.

Indeed, let us argue by induction on \(r\). When \(r=0\), it is clear since \(m=0\) and \(F\) is \(n\)-regular for every \(n\). When \(r\geq1\), we have an exact sequence \(0\to F(-1)\to F\to G\to0\) where \(G\) is a \((b_1,\dots,b_r)\)-sheaf. By 1.7 and the induction hypothesis we see that if \(n=P_{r-1}(c_1,\dots,c_r)\), then \(n\geq0\) and \(G\) is \(n\)-regular. Therefore, by 1.4 \(F\) is \([n+h^1(F(n-1))]\)-regular and \(H^q(F(n-1))=0\) for \(q\geq2\). Therefore, \(h^1(F(n-1))=h^0(F(n-1))-\chi(F(n-1))\), which is \(\leq\sum_{i=0}^r(b_i-a_i)\binom{n-1+i}{i}\) by 1.8 (i) since \(b_i\geq0\). Finally, \(F\) is \([n+\sum_{i=0}^r c_i\binom{n-1+i}{i}]\)-regular by virtue of 1.3 (i); whence the first assertion. The second follows from it thanks to 1.8 (ii) and 1.10.

1.12.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(X\) a \(S\)-scheme of finite type. Let \(\mathfrak F\) be a family of classes of coherent sheaves on the fibers of \(X/S\); that is, for every point \(s\in S\) and every extension \(K\) of \(k(s)\), we are given coherent sheaves \(F_K\) on the algebraic scheme \(X_K\), and we say that an \(F_K\) and an \(F'_{K'}\) are in the same class if there exist \(k(s)\)-homomorphisms from \(K,K'\) into a common extension \(K''\) of \(k(s)\) such that \(F_{K''}(=F_K\otimes_K K'')\) and \(F'_{K''}\) are isomorphic over \(X_{K''}\). Then the family \(\mathfrak F\) is said to be limited by the coherent sheaf \(F\) on \(X_T=X\times_S T\) where \(T\) is of finite type over \(S\), if \(\mathfrak F\) is contained in the family of the classes of the sheaves \(F_{k(t)}\), with \(t\in T\); we say that \(\mathfrak F\) is limited if there exists \(T,F\) as above.

Suppose moreover that \(X/S\) is projective and equipped with a (relatively) ample invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(1)\). Then \(\mathfrak F\) is said to be a \((b)\)-family, for a sequence of integers \((b)=(b_0,\dots,b_r)\), if every class in \(\mathfrak F\) is represented by an \(F_K\) with \(K\) algebraically closed, which is a \((b)\)-sheaf.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.13.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(X\) a projective \(S\)-scheme equipped with an ample sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(1)\) whose induced sheaves \(\mathcal O_{X_s}(1)\), with \(s\in S\), are generated by their \(H^0(\mathcal O_{X_s}(1))\); let \(\mathfrak F\) be a family of classes of coherent sheaves on the fibers of \(X/S\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(\mathfrak F\) is limited. Moreover, if all the \(F_K\in\mathfrak F\) are locally free of rank \(p\), then we may assume that \(\mathfrak F\) is limited by a sheaf \(F\) on some \(X_T\), with \(F\) locally free of rank \(p\).

(ii) The set of Hilbert polynomials \(\chi(F_K(n))\) of the \(F_K\in\mathfrak F\) is finite, and there exists a sequence of integers \((b)\) such that \(\mathfrak F\) is a \((b)\)-family.

(iii) The set of Hilbert polynomials \(\chi(F_K(n))\) of the \(F_K\in\mathfrak F\) is finite, and there exists an integer \(m\) such that all the \(F_K\in\mathfrak F\) are \(m\)-regular.

(iv) The set of Hilbert polynomials \(\chi(F_K(n))\) of the \(F_K\in\mathfrak F\) is finite, and \(\mathfrak F\) is contained in the family of the classes of quotients of sheaves of the form \(E_K\), where \(E\) is a coherent sheaf on some \(X_T\). Moreover, we may assume \(T=S\) and \(E\) of the form \(\mathcal O_X(-m)^{\oplus M}\).

\(v\) \(\mathfrak F\) is contained in the family of the classes of cokernels of homomorphisms of the form \(E'_K\to E_K\), where \(E'\) and \(E\) are coherent sheaves on some \(X_T\). Moreover, we may assume \(T=S\) and \(E'\) and \(E\) of the form \(\mathcal O_X(-m')^{\oplus M'}\) and \(\mathcal O_X(-m)^{\oplus M}\).

Indeed, suppose that \(\mathfrak F\) is limited by a sheaf \(F\) on some \(X_T\); after possibly cutting \(T\) into pieces, using the generic flatness theorem (EGA IV 6.9.1), we may assume \(F\) flat over \(T\). Then,

the number of polynomials \(\chi(\mathfrak F(n))\) is at most equal to the number of connected components of \(T\) (EGA III 7.9.5) and it is a small lemma that if for some \(t\in T\), \(F_t\) is locally free of rank \(p\), then so is \(F\) above an open neighborhood of \(t\) in \(T\), (see EGA IV 11.3.10, where one takes \(X=Y\), \(f=\mathrm{id}\)). Now, it follows from the hypotheses and from (EGA III 7.7.6) that after possibly cutting \(T\) still finer, we can find a sequence \(\sigma\) of sections \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_r\in H^0(\mathcal O_{X\times T}(1))\) which is \(F\)-regular. Therefore (ii) follows from the upper semi-continuity property of the function \(t\longmapsto h^0(F_{t,i}(-1))\) where for \(t\in T\) and \(0\leq i\leq r\), \(F_{t,i}\) denotes the restriction of \(F_t\) to the scheme of zeros of \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_i\) (EGA III 7.7.5).
The implication (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iii) follows immediately from 1.11.
The implication (iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iv) follows from 1.3 (iii) by taking \(M=\max(\chi(F_K(m)))\) and \(E=\mathcal O_X(-m)^{\oplus M}\).
Suppose that \(\mathfrak F\) satisfies (iv); there exist exact sequences
\[0\longrightarrow F'_K\longrightarrow E_K\longrightarrow F_K\longrightarrow0\]

and the set of polynomials \(\chi(F_K(n))\) is finite. By hypothesis the family of classes of the \(E_K\) is bounded; hence by the implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) already proved, the set of polynomials \(\chi(E_K(n))\) is finite and there exists a sequence of integers \((b)\) such that all the \(E_K\) (with \(K\) algebraically closed) are \((b)\)-sheaves. Consequently, the set of polynomials \(\chi(F'_K(n))\) is finite and by virtue of 1.6, all the \(F'_K\) are \((b)\)-sheaves. Hence, applying to the family of classes of the \(F'_K\) the implication (iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iv) already proved, we find \(v\).
Suppose finally that \(\mathfrak F\) satisfies \(v\); let us prove that \(\mathfrak F\) is bounded. We may assume that \(E\) (resp. \(E'\)) is of the form \(\mathcal O_X(-m)^{\oplus M}\). Indeed,

by (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iv) take a surjection \(L=\mathcal O_{X_T}(-m)^{\oplus M}\longrightarrow E\). After possibly cutting \(T\) into pieces, so that \(E\) becomes flat over \(T\), the formation of the exact sequence \(0\to I\xrightarrow{u}L\to E\to0\) commutes with passage to the fibres. By (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iii) and (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iv), take \(m_1\) large enough that all the \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(\mathcal O_{X_K}(-m_1),I_K)=H^1(I_K(m_1))\) are zero and that there exist surjections \(L_1=\mathcal O_{X_T}(-m_1)^{\oplus M_1}\longrightarrow E'\) and \(\alpha:L_2=\mathcal O_{X_T}(-m_1)^{\oplus M_2}\longrightarrow I\). Then the maps \(\operatorname{Hom}(L_{1,K},L_K)\to\operatorname{Hom}(L_{1,K},E_K)\) are surjective. Let \(\beta:E'_K\longrightarrow E_K\) be a homomorphism and let \(\gamma\) be the composition \(L'_{K}\longrightarrow E'_{K}\longrightarrow E_K\); then \(\gamma\) comes from a homomorphism \(\delta:L_{1,K}\longrightarrow L_K\), and \(\{\delta,u_K\alpha\}:L_{1,K}\oplus L_{2,K}\longrightarrow L_K\) and \(\beta:E'_K\longrightarrow E_K\) have the same cokernel. Consequently, \(\mathfrak F\) is contained in the family of classes of cokernels of homomorphisms of the form \(\mathcal O_{X_K}(-m_1)^{\oplus(M_1+M_2)}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X_K}(-m)^{\oplus M}\).

Again after possibly cutting \(T\) into pieces, we may assume in addition that \(X_T\) (hence \(\mathcal Hom_{\mathcal O_{X_T}}(E',E)\)) and all the \(R^q(f_T)_*\mathcal Hom_{\mathcal O_{X_T}}(E',E)\) (where \(f_T:X_T\to T\) is the structural morphism) are flat over \(T\), so that the formation of \(G=(f_T)_*\mathcal Hom_{\mathcal O_{X_T}}(E',E)\) commutes with arbitrary base change \(T'\to T\) (EGA III 6.9.9.2). Let \(R=V(G^*)\); then \(X_R\) canonically carries a "universal" exact sequence
\[E'_R\longrightarrow E_R\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow0\]
and \(F\) bounds \(\mathfrak F\).

footnotes.html: It is understood, here as in analogous situations in what follows, that \(F(-1)\to F\) is defined by tensoring with a given section of \(\mathcal O_X(1)\).

blocks.html: 2. Several technical lemmas

2.1. Let \(k\) be a field and \(X\) a proper \(k\)-scheme. Let \(L_1,\dots,L_s\) be invertible sheaves on \(X\), \(c_1(L_i)\) the Chern class of \(L_i\) and \(Y\) a closed subscheme of \(X\) of dimension \(\leq s\). Recall that one denotes by
\(\langle c_1(L_1)\cdots c_1(L_s),Y\rangle\) the intersection number, which can be computed as the coefficient of the monomial \(n_1\cdots n_s\) in the Snapper polynomial
\[\chi(L_1^{\otimes n_1}\otimes\cdots\otimes L_s^{\otimes n_s}\otimes\mathcal O_Y)\qquad\text{(X 4.3.1).}\]

2.2. From now on, we assume that \(k\) is algebraically closed, that \(X\) is projective, equipped with a very ample invertible sheaf \(H=\mathcal O_X(1)\), and that \(X\) is integral, of dimension \(r\).
Let \(L\) be an invertible sheaf on \(X\). The integer \(\langle c_1(L)\cdot c_1(H)^{r-1}\rangle\) is called the degree of \(L\) (relative to \(H\)) and is denoted \(d(L)\).
In what follows we consider an invertible sheaf \(L\) and the Hilbert polynomials:
\[\chi(L(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i\binom{n+i}{i}\quad\text{et}\quad \chi(\mathcal O_X(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r e_i\binom{n+i}{i}.\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.3.

We have: (i) \(a_r=e_r=d(H)\).

(ii) (If \(r\geq1\)), \(a_{r-1}-e_{r-1}=d(L)\).
Indeed, we obtain (i) and (ii) at once by setting \(m=0,1\) in the following polynomial:
\[\chi(L^{\otimes m}\otimes H^{\otimes n})=\sum_{i+j\leq r}f_{ij}\binom{m+i}{i}\binom{n+j}{j}.\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4.

If \(d(L)\leq0\) and \(h^0(L)\neq0\), then \(L\simeq\mathcal O_X\); hence \(d(L)=0\) and \(h^0(L)=1\).

Indeed, since \(X\) is integral, there exists a divisor \(Y\geq0\) such that \(L\simeq\mathcal O_X(Y)\). If \(Y>0\), we would have \(0\geq\langle c_1(L)\cdot c_1(H)^{r-1}\rangle=\langle c_1(H)^{r-1}\cdot Y\rangle\geq1\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.5.

If \(d(L)\leq0\), then \(L\) is a \((0,\dots,0,a_r)\)-sheaf (with \(r-1\) zeros).

Indeed, if \(r=0\), \(L\) is evidently a \(a_0\)-sheaf. If \(r\geq1\), by 2.4, \(h^0(L(-1))=0\) since \(d(L(-1))<0\). Let \(Y\) be a hyperplane section of \(X\) and let \(0\to L(-1)\to L\to L_Y\to0\) be an exact sequence defined by \(Y\). If \(r=1\), by 1.7, \(h^0(L_Y(-1))=a_1\); hence \(L\) is a \((0,a_1)\)-sheaf. If \(r\geq2\), we can take \(Y\) integral thanks to Bertini's theorem, and we may thus assume by induction that \(L_Y\) is a \((0,\dots,a_r)\)-sheaf; whence the assertion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.7.

If \(r=2\) and \(d(L)\geq0\), then:

(i) For every \(n\), \(h^1(L(n))\leq a_1(a_1+1)a_2-a_0\).

(ii) \(a_0\leq a_1(a_1+1)\).

Let \(Y\) be an integral hyperplane section of \(X\). By 1.7 and 2.6, \(L_Y\) is a \((0,a_2)\)-sheaf with Hilbert polynomial \(\chi(L_Y(n))=a_1+a_2\binom{n+1}{1}\). Hence by 1.11, \(-a_1\geq0\) and \(L_Y\) is \([-a_1]\)-regular.
Consider the following exact sequence:
\[H^0(L_Y(n))\longrightarrow H^1(L(n-1))\longrightarrow H^1(L(n))\longrightarrow H^1(L_Y(n)).\]

For \(n\geq-a_1-1\), we have \(h^1(L(-a_1-2))\geq h^1(L(n))\) because \(h^1(L_Y(n))=0\) by 1.3 (i); for \(n\leq-1\), we have \(h^1(L(n-1))\leq h^1(L(-1))\) because \(h^0(L_Y(n))=0\) by 2.4. Finally, for every \(n\), \(h^0(L_Y(n-1))\leq h^0(L_Y(n))\) because there exists an injection \(L_Y(n-1)\to L_Y(n)\), and \(h^0(L_Y(-a_1-1))=a_2\binom{-a_1}{1}+a_1\) because \(h^1(L_Y(-a_1-1))=0\); hence for \(n\leq-a_1-2\), we have \(h^1(L(n))-h^1(L(n+1))\leq-a_2a_1+a_1\). As a consequence, we conclude that for every \(n\),
\[\tag{2.7.1}h^1(L(n))\leq h^1(L(-a_1-2))+(-a_1-1)(-a_2a_1+a_1).\]
\[\tag{2.7.2}0\leq h^1(L(-a_1-2))\leq-a_1(-a_1-1)-a_0.\]
Finally, 2.7.2 gives (ii), and 2.7.1 and 2.7.2 give (i).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.8.

We assume that \(r=2\) and \(d(L)\leq0\). Let \(\delta=d(L)\) and \(\beta=\delta(e_2-1)+e_1\). Then:

\[a_0\geq\langle c_1(L)^2\rangle+2e_0+\sum_{i=1}^2(2e_i-a_i)\binom{\delta-1+i}{i}+\beta(\beta+1).\]

Indeed, let \(\chi(L^{\otimes m}(\delta-1))=\alpha_2m^2+\alpha_1m+\alpha_0\); then it follows by comparing polynomials that \(2\alpha_2=\langle c_1(L)^2\rangle\), that \(\alpha_0=\sum_{i=0}^2e_i\binom{\delta-1+i}{i}\) and that \(\alpha_2+\alpha_1+\alpha_0=\sum_{i=0}^2a_i\binom{\delta-1+i}{i}\). Let \(\chi(L^{-1}(\delta)(n))=\sum_{i=0}^2\beta_i\binom{n+i}{i}\); then \(\beta_0=2\alpha_2-\alpha_1+\alpha_0\) and by 2.3, \(\beta_1=d(L^{-1}(\delta))+e_1\), which equals \(\beta\). Hence \(a_0=2\alpha_2+2\alpha_0-\sum_{i=1}^2a_i\binom{\delta-1+i}{i}-\beta_0\) and since \(d(L^{-1}(\delta))\geq0\), we have \(\beta_0\leq\beta(\beta+1)\) by 2.7 (ii); whence the assertion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.9.

We assume that \(r\geq2\), that \(d(L)\leq0\) and that \(X\) is normal. Then:

(i) \(h^1(L(-n))=0\) for \(n\geq2+h_2(a_{r-2},a_{r-1},a_r)\),

(ii) \(h^1(L(n))\leq h_r(a_{r-2},a_{r-1},a_r)\) for every \(n\),

(iii) \(|a_i|\leq g_{r-i}(a_{r-2},a_{r-1},a_r)\) for \(i=0,\dots,r\),

where \(h_2(X_0,X_1,X_2),h_3(X_0,X_1,X_2),\dots\) and \(g_0(X_0,X_1,X_2),g_1(X_0,X_1,X_2),\dots\) are universal polynomials with coefficients in \(\mathbb Q\), which depend neither on \(k\) nor on \(X\).
Indeed, let us first treat the case \(r=2\). Let \(g_i(X_0,X_1,X_2)=X_{2-i}^2\) for \(i=0,1,2\) and \(h_2(X_0,X_1,X_2)=X_1(X_1+1)X_2-X_0\). Then (iii) is evident and (ii) is 2.7 (i).

Let \(X\) be in \(P=\mathbb P_k^N\) and \(\omega=\mathcal Ext_{\mathcal O_P}^{N-2}(\mathcal O_X,\Omega_{P/k}^N)\). Then \(h^1(L(-n))=h^1(\omega\otimes L^{-1}(n))\) and \(\omega\) is invertible outside the finite set \(Z\) of singular points of \(X\) (see FGA 149 § 6). Let \(Y\) be a normal hyperplane section of \(X\) which does not contain \(Z\). Then \(\omega_Y(1)\simeq\Omega_{Y/k}^1\) and we have an exact sequence:
\[0\longrightarrow\omega\otimes L^{-1}(-1)\longrightarrow\omega\otimes L^{-1}\longrightarrow\Omega_{Y/k}^1\otimes L_Y^{-1}(-1)\longrightarrow0.\]

Now, \(h^1(\Omega_{Y/k}^1\otimes L^{-1}(n))=h^0(L_Y(-n))\) for every \(n\), and \(h^0(L_Y(-n))=0\) for \(n\geq1\) by 2.5. Hence \(\Omega_{Y/k}^1\otimes L_Y^{-1}(-1)\) is 3-regular and by 1.4, \(\omega\otimes L^{-1}\) is \([3+h^1(\omega\otimes L^{-1}(2))]\)-regular. Consequently, \(h^1(L(-n))=0\) for \(n\geq2+h^1(L(-2))\); whence (i).
Suppose now that \(r\geq3\) and let us argue by induction on \(r\). Let \(Y\) be a normal hyperplane section of \(X\) and consider the exact sequence:
\[\tag{2.9.1}H^0(L_Y(-n))\longrightarrow H^1(L(-n-1))\longrightarrow H^1(L(-n))\longrightarrow H^1(L_Y(-n)).\]

By 1.7, \(\chi(L_Y(n))=\sum_{i=0}^{r-1}a_{i+1}\binom{n+i}{i}\). Hence for \(n\geq2+h_2(a_{r-2},a_{r-1},a_r)\), the induction hypothesis gives \(h^1(L_Y(-n))=0\), and since \(n\geq2\) by (ii) for \(r=2\), 2.4 gives \(h^0(L_Y(-n))=0\), whence \(h^1(L(-n-1))=h^1(L(-n))\). But, for \(n\gg0\), \(h^1(L(n))=0\) (see FGA 149 § 6); whence (i).
As induction hypothesis, we assume that the polynomials \(g_i\) and \(h_i\) are defined for \(i\leq r-1\). Then for every \(n\),
\[h^1(L(n))\leq h^1(L(n-1))+h_{r-1}(a_{r-2},a_{r-1},a_r)\quad\text{grâce à 2.9.1.}\]
Hence by (i)
already proved, we conclude that for any \(m\geq-2\),
\[\tag{2.9.2}h^1(L(n))\leq[2+h_2(a_{r-2},a_{r-1},a_r)+m]h_{r-1}(a_{r-2},a_{r-1},a_r)\]
for every \(n\leq m\).

By 2.5, \(L_Y\) is a \((0,\dots,0,a_r)\)-sheaf. Let \(c_i=g_{r-1}(a_{r-2},a_{r-1},a_r)\) for \(i=1,\dots,r\) and \(m=P_{r-2}(c_1,\dots,c_{r-1})\). By 1.11, \(m\geq0\) and \(L_Y\) is \(m\)-regular. Hence by 1.4 (ii), \(h^1(L(m-2))\geq h^1(L(n))\) for every \(n\geq m-1\). Hence if we define polynomials by
\[M=P_{r-2}(g_{r-1},\dots,g_1)\quad\text{et}\quad h_r=(h_2+M)h_{r-1},\]
then we have (ii) by virtue of 2.9.2.

Finally, since \(L_Y\) is \(m\)-regular, \(h^q(L(m))=0\) for \(q\geq2\) by 1.4 (i); whence \(\sum_{i=0}^r a_i\binom{m+i}{i}=h^0(L(m))-h^1(L(m))\). But \(h^0(L(m))\leq a_r\binom{m+r}{r}\) by 1.8 (i). Hence if we define \(g_r=\sum_{i=1}^r g_{r-i}\binom{M+i}{i}+a_r\binom{M+r}{r}+h_r\), we have (iii).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.10.

For fixed \(m\), consider an equality of polynomials in \(n\):

\[\sum_{i=0}^r a_{m,i}\binom{n+i}{i}=\sum_{j=0}^r a_j\binom{mn+m+j}{j}.\]

Then one easily finds the formula

\[a_{m,i}=\sum_{j=0}^{r-i}a_{j+i}\binom{m-1+j}{j}\]

by arguing by induction on \(r\) so that \(a_{m,i}\) becomes \(a_{m,0}\), and by setting \(n=-1\).

Similarly, one finds that there exists a polynomial with rational coefficients \(P_i^{(m,r)}(X_i,\dots,X_r)\), increasing in \(X_i\), such that in the following equality:

\[\sum_{i=0}^r a_i^{(m)}\binom{n+i}{i}=\sum_{j=0}^r a_j\binom{mn+j}{j}\]

we have: \(a_i^{(m)}=P_i^{(m,r)}(a_i,\dots,a_r)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.11.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(X\) a projective \(S\)-scheme, equipped with a very ample sheaf \(H=\mathcal O_X(1)\). We assume that the fibres of \(X/S\) are geometrically integral, of the same dimension \(r\). Let \(\mathfrak L\) be a family of classes of invertible sheaves \(L_K\) on the fibres of \(X/S\). Then for \(\mathfrak L\) to be bounded, it is (necessary and) sufficient that in the Hilbert polynomials \(\chi(L_K(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i\binom{n+i}{i}\) of the \(L_K\in\mathfrak L\), all the coefficients \(a_i\) remain bounded. Moreover, if the fibres of \(X/S\) are also geometrically normal, then it suffices that the three coefficients \(a_r,a_{r-1},a_{r-2}\) remain bounded.

Indeed, let \(\delta=\sup\{d(L_K)\}\). If \(\delta>0\), we may replace \(\mathfrak L\) by the family of classes of the \(L_K(-\delta)\) thanks to 2.3 and 2.10; we may thus assume \(\delta\leq0\). Then \(\mathfrak L\) is a \((b)\)-family by 2.5, hence bounded if all the coefficients \(a_i\) remain bounded, by virtue of 1.13, and, in the case where the fibres of \(X/S\) are geometrically normal, it suffices for this that the three coefficients \(a_r,a_{r-1},a_{r-2}\) remain bounded, by virtue of 2.9.
3. General finiteness theorems

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1.

Let \(S\) be a scheme and \(X\) a quasi-compact and quasi-separated \(S\)-scheme; let \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) be the Picard functor, (localized for the f.p.p.f. topology)(*). Then for every filtered projective system \((Z_\alpha)\) of \(S\)-schemes

which are quasi-compact and quasi-separated, with affine transition morphisms, the canonical map

\[\tag{3.1.1}\underset{\longrightarrow}{\lim}\,\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}(Z_\alpha)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}(\underset{\longleftarrow}{\lim}\,Z_\alpha)\]

is bijective.

Consequently (EGA IV 8.14.2), if the Picard functor is represented by a \(S\)-scheme \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\), then \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) is locally of finite presentation.

Indeed, an element \(\lambda_\alpha\) of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}(Z_\alpha)\) is represented by an invertible sheaf \(L'_\alpha\) on some \(X_{Z'_\alpha}\) where \(Z'_\alpha\to Z_\alpha\) is an f.p.p.f. morphism. Set \(Z=\underset{\longleftarrow}{\lim}Z_\alpha\). The image \(\lambda\) of \(\lambda_\alpha\) in \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}(Z)\) is represented by the invertible sheaf \(L'\) on \(X_{Z'}\), the inverse image of \(L'_\alpha\), where \(Z'=Z'_\alpha\times_{Z_\alpha}Z\).

If \(\lambda\) is the neutral element of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}(Z)\), there exists an f.p.p.f. morphism \(Z''\to Z'\) such that the inverse image of \(L'\) on \(X_{Z''}\) is \(\widetilde\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X_{Z''}}\). By virtue of (EGA IV 8.8.2, 8.10.5 (vi), 11.2.6 (ii), 8.5.2 (i), and 8.5.2.4), there exist a \(\beta\geq\alpha\) and an f.p.p.f. morphism \(Z''_\beta\to Z'_\beta\) where \(Z'_\beta=Z'_\alpha\times_{Z_\alpha}Z_\beta\) such that the inverse image of \(L'_\alpha\) on \(X_{Z''_\beta}\) is \(\widetilde\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{X_{Z''_\beta}}\). The image of \(\lambda_\alpha\) is therefore already the neutral element in \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}(Z_\beta)\). Consequently 3.1.1 is injective.
The surjectivity of 3.1.1 is proved in an analogous way.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.2.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(X\) a proper \(S\)-scheme. We assume that the Picard functor is represented by a \(S\)-scheme \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\). Then:

(i) \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) is locally of finite type over \(S\).

(ii) The subsets \(\Lambda\) of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) correspond bijectively to the families \(\mathfrak L\) of classes of invertible sheaves on the fibres of \(X/S\).

(iii) A family \(\Lambda\) of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) is quasi-compact if and only if the corresponding family \(\mathfrak L\) is bounded.

Indeed, (i) follows from (3.1); (ii), from the following fact: if \(s\) is a point of \(S\) and \(K\) is an algebraically closed extension of \(k(s)\), then the \(K\)-points of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) correspond bijectively to the isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves on \(X_K\).

As for (iii), suppose that the subset \(\Lambda\) of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) is quasi-compact. Then \(\Lambda\) is contained in an open subset \(U\) of finite type, and the inclusion \(U\to\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) corresponds to an invertible sheaf \(L\) on some \(X_T\), where \(T\to U\) is a surjective, (flat) morphism of finite type. Finally \(T\to S\) is of finite type, hence \(L\) bounds \(\mathfrak L\).

Conversely, suppose that \(\mathfrak L\) is bounded by a coherent sheaf \(L\) on some \(X_T\), with \(T\) of finite type over \(S\). We may assume \(L\) invertible (1.13 (i)) so that \(L\) defines a morphism \(f:T\to\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\). The image of \(f\) contains \(\Lambda\) and is quasi-compact, hence noetherian by (i). Consequently \(\Lambda\) is quasi-compact and we have (iii).
Thanks to 3.2, the usual definition of morphisms of finite type between two Picard schemes generalizes to the following:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.3.

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be proper over the noetherian scheme \(S\). A morphism between the Picard functors

\[\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/S}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\]
is said to be of finite type if every bounded family of classes of invertible sheaves on the fibres of \(X/S\) has as inverse image a bounded family of classes of sheaves on the fibres of \(Y/S\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4.

Let \(X\), \(Y\), \(X'\), \(Y'\) be proper over the noetherian scheme \(S\) and \(T\to S\) surjective of finite type. Then:

\(i\) For a morphism \(\Phi:\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/S}\to\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) to be of finite type, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\Phi_T:\operatorname{Pic}_{Y_T/T}\to\operatorname{Pic}_{X_T/T}\) be of finite type.

\(ii\) In a commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix{ {\Phi':\operatorname{Pic}_{Y'/S}}\ar[r]&{\operatorname{Pic}_{X'/S}}\\ {\Phi:\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/S}}\ar[r]\ar[u]^{\Phi_Y}&{\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}}\ar[u]_{\Phi_X} }\]

with \(\Phi'\) and \(\Phi_Y\) of finite type, \(\Phi\) is of finite type.

Indeed, suppose \(\Phi_T\) is of finite type. Let \(\mathfrak L\) be a family on the fibres of \(X/S\), bounded by a \(L\) on some \(X_R\). The family \(\mathfrak L_T=\mathfrak L\times_S T\) on the fibres of \(X_T/T\), defined in the evident way, is bounded by the inverse image of \(L\) on \(X_{R\times T}\). If \(\Phi_T^{-1}(\mathfrak L_T)\) is bounded by a \(M\) on some \(Y_Q\), then \(M\) also bounds \(\Phi^{-1}(\mathfrak L)\).
The converse of \(i\) and the assertion \(ii\) are proved in an analogous way.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.5.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(X\) and \(Y\) two proper \(S\)-schemes and \(f:X\to Y\) a morphism. If \(f\) is surjective, then the induced morphism between the Picard functors

\[f^*:\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/S}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\]

is of finite type.

Indeed, we may evidently assume \(S\) reduced and irreducible by 3.4 \(i\). Then there exists a non-empty open subset \(U\) of \(S\) such that \(\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/S}\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) are representable and the restriction of \(f^*\) is quasi-affine (XII 1.1), hence of finite type by 3.2 \(i\). Equipping \(T=S-U\) with the induced reduced structure, we may assume that the morphism \(\operatorname{Pic}_{Y_T/T}\to\operatorname{Pic}_{X_T/T}\) induced by \(f^*\) is of finite type by noetherian induction; whence the assertion, thanks to 3.4 \(i\) applied to \(U\amalg T\to S\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.6.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(X\) a proper \(S\)-scheme and \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) the Picard functor. Then for every integer \(n\ne0\), the \(n\)-th power homomorphism

\[\Phi_n:\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\]

is a morphism of finite type.

Indeed, let \(f:X'\to X\) be a Chow presentation: \(X'\) is projective over \(S\) and \(f\) is surjective. If we grant the theorem for \(X'\), then by virtue of 3.5 and 3.4 \(ii\), the theorem for \(X\) follows. We may therefore assume \(X/S\) projective.

There exist a non-empty open subset \(U\) of \(S\), an integral scheme \(S'\) and a finite surjective morphism \(S'\to U\), such that every irreducible component \(X'_i\) of \(X_{S'}\), equipped with the induced reduced structure, is integral over \(S'\) (XII 1.3). Equipping \(T=S-U\) with the induced reduced structure, we may grant the theorem for \(X_T/T\) by noetherian induction. If we grant it for all the \(X'_i/S'\), it follows for \(\coprod X'_i/S'\) by the
following easy lemma, hence for \(X_{S'}/S'\) by virtue of 3.5 and 3.4 \(ii\), and finally for \(X/S\) thanks to 3.4 \(i\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.7.

Let \(X=\coprod X_i\) be of finite type over the noetherian scheme \(S\) and \(\mathfrak F\) a family of classes of coherent sheaves on the fibres of \(X/S\). For \(\mathfrak F\) to be bounded, it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(i\) the family \(\mathfrak F_i\) induced on the fibres of \(X_i/S\) be bounded.

In the proof of 3.6, we reduced to the case where \(X\) is projective and integral over \(S\); let us equip \(X/S\) with a very ample sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(1)\). Let \(\mathfrak L\) be a bounded family of classes of invertible sheaves on the fibres of \(X/S\) and \(L\) an invertible sheaf on some \(X_T\) with \(T\) of finite type over \(S\), which bounds \(\mathfrak L\). After possibly cutting \(T\) into pieces, we may assume \(L\) flat over \(T\), and after possibly replacing \(T\) by each of its connected components, we may assume \(T\) connected. Then the polynomial in \(q\) and \(m\), \(P(q,m)=\chi(L_t^{\otimes q}(m))\) does not depend on \(t\in T\) (EGA III 7.9.4).
Let \(\mathfrak L_n=\Phi_n^{-1}(\mathfrak L)\): \(\mathfrak L_n\) is the family of the classes of the invertible sheaves \(M_K\) such that the class of \(M_K^{\otimes n}\) is in \(\mathfrak L\). Let
\[\chi(M_K^{\otimes p}(m))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i(p){m+i\choose i},\]

where \(a_i(p)\) is a polynomial in \(p\) of degree \(\leq r-i\). For \(q,m\) varying, we have \(\chi(M_K^{\otimes qn}(m))=P(q,m)\) and each \(a_i(qn)\) therefore does not depend on the \(M_K\) chosen; consequently each polynomial \(a_i(p)\) does not depend on \(M_K\) either. Consequently the family \(\mathfrak L_n\) has a single Hilbert polynomial \(\chi(M_K(m))\), hence is bounded by virtue of 2.11.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.8.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(Y\) a projective \(S\)-scheme equipped with a relatively ample invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_Y(1)\), \(X\) the scheme of zeros of a section \(\varphi\) of \(\mathcal O_Y(1)\), and \(f:X\to Y\) the inclusion morphism. We

assume that for every point \(s\in S\), and every irreducible component \(Y_s^i\) of \(Y_s\), the irreducible components of \(X\cap Y_s^i=V(\varphi|Y_s^i)\) are of dimension \(\geq2\), (which is the case if the irreducible components of the fibres of \(Y\) are of dimension \(\geq3\)). Then the induced morphism between the Picard functors

\[f^*:\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/S}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\]

is of finite type.

Moreover, if \(S\) is integral, there exists a non-empty open subset \(U\) of \(S\) such that the Picard functors restricted to \(U\) are representable and the induced morphism

\[\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/S}|U\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}|U\]

is quasi-affine (of finite type).

Let us first prove the first assertion. As in the proof of 3.6, one sees by noetherian induction that we may replace \(S\) by any non-empty open subset, and we easily reduce to the case where \(S\) is integral and \(Y\) is integral over \(S\), always replacing \(X\) by \(X\times_Y Y'\) when we replace \(Y\) by \(Y'\); this done, we reduce in the same way to the case where \(Y\) is normal over \(S\), using the following lemma in place of XII 1.3.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.9.

Let \(S\) be an integral noetherian scheme and \(X\) a \(S\)-scheme, integral over \(S\). Then there exist a non-empty open subset \(U\) of \(S\), an integral scheme \(S'\) finite and faithfully flat over \(U\), a \(S'\)-scheme \(X'\) normal and integral over \(S'\), and a finite surjective \(S'\)-morphism \(X'\to X_{S'}\).

Indeed, let \(\eta\) be the generic point of \(S\) and \(K\) the algebraic closure of \(k(\eta)\). It is clear that \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)\) is the limit of the filtered projective system (\(S_\alpha\)) of integral affine schemes equipped with a finite and faithfully flat morphism \(S_\alpha\to U_\alpha\) where \(U_\alpha\) is a non-empty open subset of \(S\). The normalization of \(X_K\) therefore comes from a \(S_\alpha\)-scheme \(X'\) for some \(\alpha\), and the finite surjective morphism \(X'_K\to X_K\) from a finite surjective \(S'_\alpha\)-morphism \(X'\to X_{S'_\alpha}\) (EGA IV 8.8.2, 8.10.5). Finally, since \(X'_K\) is normal and integral, there exists an open subset \(S'\) of \(S_\alpha\) such that \(X'_{S'}\) is normal and integral over \(S'\) (EGA IV 9.9.5).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.10.

Keeping the notation and conditions of 3.9, suppose that there exists a resolving morphism \(X'_K\to X_K\) (EGA IV 7.9.1), (for example if (\dim\(X_K\)\leq2), by Abhyankar). Then a similar argument shows that there exist a finite and faithfully flat morphism \(S'\to U\), a scheme \(X'\) smooth over \(S'\) and a proper surjective birational morphism \(X'\to X_{S'}\).

In the proof of the first part of 3.8, we reduced to the case where \(S\) is integral and \(Y\) is normal and integral over \(S\), so \(Y\) is integral. If \(\varphi=0\), then \(X=Y\) and the assertion is trivial. Otherwise, after possibly shrinking \(S\), we may assume that for every \(s\in S\), we have \(\varphi_s\ne0\), hence \(\varphi_s\) is \(\mathcal O_{Y_s}\)-regular and the dimension \(r\) of \(Y_s\) is \(\geq3\).

Then let \(\mathfrak L\) be a bounded family of classes of invertible sheaves on the fibres of \(X/S\). Let \(M_K\) be an invertible sheaf on a geometric fibre \(Y_K\) of \(Y/S\), with the Hilbert polynomial
\[\chi(M_K(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i{n+i\choose i},\]
then the Hilbert polynomial of \(f^*M_K\) is \(\sum_{i=0}^{r-1}a_{i+1}{n+i\choose i}\). When the class of \(M_K\) runs through \((f^*)^{-1}(\mathfrak L)\), i.e. the
class of \(f^*M_K\) is in \(\mathfrak L\), the three coefficients \(a_r\), \(a_{r-1}\), \(a_{r-2}\) remain bounded thanks to 1.13.

Let \(H=\mathcal O_Y(m)\) be a multiple of \(\mathcal O_Y(1)\) which is very ample. Then in the Hilbert polynomials \(\chi(M_K(mn))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i^{(m)}{n+i\choose i}\) relative to \(H\) of the \(M_K\) in \((f^*)^{-1}(\mathfrak L)\), the three coefficients \(a_r^{(m)}\), \(a_{r-1}^{(m)}\), \(a_{r-2}^{(m)}\) remain bounded thanks to 2.10. Consequently \(\mathfrak L\) is bounded by virtue of 2.11; whence the first part of 3.8.

As for the second part of 3.8, the "generic" representability of the Picard functors was established in XII 1.2, as well as their separation. By an argument similar to that of XII 2, using 3.9 and XII 1.1, we reduce to the case \(Y\) normal over \(S\). Now, for the morphism \(\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/S}\to\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) to be quasi-affine, it suffices that it be (separated and) quasi-finite (EGA IV 8.12.8), which can be checked on the geometric fibres, and hence follows from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.11.

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(Y\) a normal, projective \(k\)-scheme equipped with an ample sheaf \(\mathcal O_Y(1)\), and \(X\) the scheme of zeros of a section \(\varphi\) of \(\mathcal O_Y(1)\). We assume that \(Y\) is of dimension \(\geq3\) (resp. \(\geq2\)). Then the morphism \(\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/k}\to\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}\) (resp. the morphism \(\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/k}^{\tau}\to\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}^{\tau}\) (see 4.1)) is of finite type and has a kernel \(N\) which is a finite group (non-reduced in general).

Indeed, we may evidently assume \(\varphi\ne0\); hence \(\varphi\) is regular, \(Y\) being integral. The morphism in question is of finite type thanks to the first part of 3.8 (resp. thanks to 4.7 (iii)). Consequently the kernel \(N\) is an algebraic group, hence contained in \(\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/k}^{\tau}\), hence is the same

in the two cases considered. Since \(\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/k}^{\tau}\) is proper, \(Y\) being normal (FGA 236-2.13), and since \(N\) is a subgroup, necessarily closed (SGA 3 VIB 1.9.2), for \(N\) to be finite, it suffices that it not contain the subgroup of type \(\mu_\ell\) (= \(\mathbf Z/\ell\mathbf Z\)) where \((\ell,\operatorname{car}(k))=1\).
Consider Kummer theory, that is, the following commutative diagram, coming from the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\mu_\ell\longrightarrow\mathbf G_m\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{x\mapsto x^\ell}}\mathbf G_m\longrightarrow0\]
of étale sheaves on \(Y\) (resp. on \(X\))

\[\xymatrix{ &&H^1(Y,\mu_\ell)\ar[r]\ar[d]&H^1(Y,\mathbf G_m)\ar[r]^{\ell}\ar[d]&H^1(Y,\mathbf G_m)\ar[d]\\ H^0(X,\mathbf G_m)\ar[r]^{\ell}&H^0(X,\mathbf G_m)\ar[r]&H^1(X,\mu_\ell)\ar[r]&H^1(X,\mathbf G_m)\ar[r]^{\ell}&H^1(X,\mathbf G_m). }\]

By Hilbert's "Theorem 90", we have \(H^1(Y,\mathbf G_m)=\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/k}(k)\) (resp. \(H^1(X,\mathbf G_m)=\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}(k)\)). Since \(X\) is proper and integral, we have \(H^0(X,\mathbf G_m)=k^*\); hence \(H^1(X,\mu_\ell)\to H^1(X,\mathbf G_m)\) is injective.

It therefore suffices to see that \(H^1(Y,\mathbf Z/\ell\mathbf Z)\to H^1(X,\mathbf Z/\ell\mathbf Z)\) is injective, which follows from the fact that \(\pi_1(X,\xi)\to\pi_1(Y,\xi)\) is surjective, or again (SGA 1 V 6.9) that every connected étale covering \(\widetilde Y\) of \(Y\) with group \(\mathbf Z/\ell\mathbf Z\) has a restriction \(\widetilde X=X\times_Y\widetilde Y\) which is also connected, which is a well-known consequence of Bertini's theorem and of Zariski's connectedness theorem (SGA 1 X 2.10).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.12.

Let us keep the conditions of 3.11. Then:

(i) The kernel \(N\) is a finite unipotent group (SGA 3 XVII 1.1), since it does not contain the subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mu_n\) for any \(n\) (SGA 3 XVII 4.6.1 vi), which is proved in the same way, using in

place of Kummer theory the following fact, due to Grothendieck (SGA 1 XI, on the last page): if \(X\) is a proper scheme over a field \(k\), with \(H^0(X,\mathcal O_X)=k\), then there exists an isomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathrm{gr}}(\mu_n,\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k})\simeq H^1(X,\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)\); consequently, if \(k\) is algebraically closed, we have \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr}}(\mu_n,\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k})\simeq H^1(X,\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)\).

(ii) If \(k\) is of characteristic zero, then the kernel \(N\) is zero, since every finite unipotent group is then zero (SGA 3 XVII 1.7). We thus recover more or less the "Vanishing theorem" of Kodaira in the form given by Mumford [2].

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.13.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(X\) a projective \(S\)-scheme, equipped with a relatively ample invertible sheaf \(H=\mathcal O_X(1)\). We assume that the geometric fibres of \(X/S\) are integral and of dimension \(r\). Then for a family \(\mathfrak L\) of classes of invertible sheaves on the fibres of \(X/S\), the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(\mathfrak L\) is a bounded family.

(ii) In the Hilbert polynomials \(\chi(L_K(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i{n+i\choose i}\) of the \(L_K\in\mathfrak L\), the coefficient \(a_{r-1}\) remains bounded and the coefficient \(a_{r-2}\) remains bounded below.

(iii) When \(L_K\) runs through \(\mathfrak L\), the degree \(d(L_K)=\langle c_1(L_K)\cdot c_1(H_K)^{r-1}\rangle\) remains bounded and the intersection number \(\langle c_1(L_K)^2\cdot c_1(H_K)^{r-2}\rangle\) remains bounded below.

(iv) There exists an integer \(N\) such that we have

\[H^{-N}<\mathfrak L<H^N\]

in the sense of the order relation defined by the ampleness relation; that is, for every \(L_K\in\mathfrak L\), \(H_K^{\otimes N}\otimes L_K\) and \(H_K^{\otimes N}\otimes L_K^{-1}\) are ample.

Let us prove that (i) implies (ii), (iii), and (iv). Suppose then that \(\mathfrak L\) is bounded by an invertible sheaf on some \(X_T\). Then \(\mathfrak L\) satisfies (iv) by EGA II 4.6.12. Suppose \(L\) flat over \(T\). Then the polynomial in \(m\) and \(n\), \(\chi(L_t^{\otimes m}(n))\) depends only on the connected component of \(T\) (EGA III 7.9.4). Consequently \(\mathfrak L\) satisfies (ii), and, taking 2.1 into account, (iii).
Note next that the family of classes of the \(H_K\) (resp. \(\mathcal O_{X_K}\)) is bounded, hence satisfies (iii) (resp. (ii)).

Suppose that \(\mathfrak L\) satisfies (iv). Then \(\langle[Nc_1(H_K)+c_1(L_K)]\cdot c_1(H_K)^{r-1}\rangle>0\), whence \(d(L_K)>-Nd(H_K)\), and similarly, \(Nd(H_K)>d(L_K)\). Moreover, \(\langle[Nc_1(H_K)+c_1(L_K)]^2\cdot c_1(H_K)^{r-2}\rangle>0\), whence \(\langle c_1(L_K)^2\cdot c_1(H_K)^{r-2}\rangle>-2Nd(L_K)-N^2d(H_K)\). Hence \(\mathfrak L\) satisfies (iii).

Let us finally prove that (ii) (resp. (iii)) implies (i). Thanks to 2.10 and 2.3, we may replace \(H\) by a multiple; suppose then \(H\) very ample. Evidently we may cut \(S\) into pieces; suppose then that there exists a linear section \(Y\) of \(X\), which is integral over \(S\), with fibres of dimension 2. By virtue of 3.8, we may replace \(X,\mathfrak L\) by \(Y,\mathfrak L|Y\), (taking into account 1.7 (resp. the projection formula), which implies that condition (ii) (resp. (iii)) on \(X,\mathfrak L\) is equivalent to (ii) (resp. (iii)) on \(Y,\mathfrak L|Y\); suppose then \(r=2\). If \(\delta=\sup\{d(L_K)\}\) is \(>0\), we may replace \(\mathfrak L\) by the family of classes of the \(L_K(-\delta)\) thanks to 2.3 and 2.10; suppose then \(\delta\leq0\).

If in the Hilbert polynomials \(\sum_{i=0}^2 a_i{n+i\choose i}\), \(a_1\) remains bounded and \(a_0\) remains bounded below, then the three coefficients remain bounded thanks to 2.3 and 2.7; hence \(\mathfrak L\) is bounded by virtue of 2.11; thus (ii) implies
(i). On the other hand, (iii) implies (ii) thanks to 2.3 and 2.8.

footnotes.html: Cf. SGA 3 IV 6.3, and FGA n° 232 for the definition of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\), where it is appropriate here, as indicated, to replace the fpqc topology by the fppf topology.

blocks.html: 4. Finiteness theorems for \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau}\)

4.1. Let \(S\) be a scheme and \(G\) a commutative group functor on \(S\). We denote by \(G^0\) (resp. \(G^{\tau}\)) the subgroup functor whose \(T\)-points \(f:T\to G\) are those of \(G\) such that for every algebraically closed field \(K\) and every \(K\)-point \(t\) of \(T\), there exist a connected \(K\)-scheme \(Z\), two \(K\)-points \(z,z_0\) of \(Z\) and a morphism \(g:Z\to G\) such that \(g(z)=f(t)\) and \(g(z_0)=0\) in \(G(K)\) (resp. there exists a non-zero integer \(n\) such that \(n.f(t)\in G^0(K)\)).

It is evident that the formation of \(G^0\) (resp. \(G^{\tau}\)) commutes with any base change, that a morphism of group functors \(G\to G'\) induces a morphism \(G^0\to G'^0\) (resp. \(G^{\tau}\to G'^{\tau}\)), and that if \(S\) is a field and \(G\) is represented by a group scheme locally of finite type, then \(G^0\) is represented by the connected component of the neutral element, which is an open (and closed) subscheme, of finite type, and geometrically irreducible (SGA 3 VIA 2.4).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2.

Let \(\Phi:G\to G'\) be a morphism between two commutative group functors on a scheme \(S\). We assume that for every algebraically closed field \(K\) and every morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)\to S\), \(G_K\) (resp. \(G'_K\)) is representable by a group scheme locally of finite type, and \(\Phi_K\) is representable by a morphism of finite type, (i.e. quasi-compact). Then

\[\Phi^{-1}(G'^{\tau})=G^{\tau}.\]

Indeed, we have already noted the inclusion \(\Phi(G'^{\tau})\supset G^{\tau}\). For the inclusion in the opposite direction, we note that \(g\in G(K)\) is in \(G^{\tau}(K)\) if and only if the sequence of the \(ng\,(n\in\mathbb N)\) is quasi-compact; since \(\Phi_K\) is quasi-compact, it suffices for this that the sequence of the \(\Phi(ng)=n\Phi(g)\) have the same property; whence the assertion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3.

Let \(X,Y\) be proper over a scheme \(S\) and \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a surjective morphism (resp. the inclusion morphism of the scheme of zeros of a section \(\varphi\) of an ample sheaf \(\mathcal O_Y(1)\)). Then

\[(f^*)^{-1}\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau}=\operatorname{Pic}_{Y/S}^{\tau}.\]
Indeed, the assertion is a formal consequence of 4.2, (XII 1.2), 3.1, and 3.5 (resp. 3.8).

4.4. Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(X\) a proper \(k\)-scheme, and \(L\) an invertible sheaf on \(X\). We say that \(L\) is algebraically equivalent to 0 (resp. \(\tau\)-equivalent to 0) if there exist a connected \(k\)-scheme of finite type \(T\), an invertible sheaf \(M\) on \(X_T=X\times_kT\), and two \(k\)-points \(t,t_0\) of \(T\) such that \(L\simeq M_t\) and \(\mathcal O_X\simeq M_{t_0}\) (resp. if there exists a non-zero integer \(n\) such that \(L^{\otimes n}\) is algebraically equivalent to 0), or what easily amounts to the same thing, if the \(k\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}\) which corresponds to \(L\) is in \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}^0(k)\) (resp. in \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}^{\tau}(k)\)).
Recall also that \(L\) is said to be numerically equivalent to 0 if for every closed integral curve \(Y\) in \(X\), the intersection number \(\langle c_1(L).Y\rangle\) is 0 (see 2.1 or X 4.5).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5. (Functorial behaviour)

Let \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) be a morphism between two proper \(k\)-schemes, and let \(L\) be an invertible module on \(X\). Then:

(i) If \(L\) is \(\tau\)-equivalent to 0 (resp. numerically equivalent to 0), then so is \(f^*L\).

(ii) Conversely, when \(f\) is surjective, if \(f^*L\) is \(\tau\)-equivalent to 0 (resp. numerically equivalent to 0), then so is \(L\).

Indeed, assertion (i) (resp. (ii)) for \(\tau\)-equivalence follows at once from 4.1 (resp. 4.3). On the other hand, let \(Y'\) be a closed integral curve in \(X'\), and \(Y=f(Y')\) its image, equipped with the induced reduced structure. When \(\dim(Y)=1\), we have the projection formula (X 4.4.4)
\[(4.5.1)\qquad \langle c_1(f^*L).Y'\rangle=[k(Y'):k(Y)]\,\langle c_1(L).Y\rangle\]

where \(k(Y')\) (resp. \(k(Y)\)) is the function field of \(Y'\) (resp. \(Y\)), and when \(\dim(Y)=0\), we have \((f^*L)_{Y'}\simeq\mathcal O_{Y'}\). Consequently, if \(L\) is numerically equivalent to 0, then \(\langle c_1(f^*L).Y'\rangle=0\); whence (i).

Conversely, when \(f\) is surjective, given a closed integral curve \(Y\) in \(X\), one can find a closed integral curve \(Y'\) in \(X'\) such that \(Y=f(Y')\): let \(y\) be the generic point of \(Y\), \(y'\) a point of the fibre \(f^{-1}(y)\) algebraic over \(k(y)\), and \(Y'\) the closure of \(y'\). Then 4.5.1 shows that \(\langle c_1(f^*L).Y'\rangle=0\) implies \(\langle c_1(L).Y\rangle=0\); whence (ii).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.6. (The numerical characterization of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}^{\tau}\))

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(X\) a proper \(k\)-scheme, and \(L\) an invertible sheaf on \(X\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(L\) is \(\tau\)-equivalent to 0.

(ii) For every coherent sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), we have

\[\chi(F\otimes L)=\chi(F)\]

(iii) \(L\) is numerically equivalent to 0.

If \(X/k\) is projective and equipped with an ample invertible module \(H=\mathcal O_X(1)\), the preceding conditions are also equivalent to the following:

(iv) For every integer \(m\) (positive or negative), \(L^{\otimes m}(1)\) is ample.

\(v\) (If \(X\) is irreducible). For every pair of integers \(m,n\), we have

\[\chi(L^{\otimes m}(n))=\chi(\mathcal O_X(n))\]

(vi) (If \(X\) is irreducible of dimension \(r\geq2\)). We have

\[\langle c_1(L)\cdot c_1(H)^{r-1}\rangle=0,\quad \langle c_1(L)^2\cdot c_1(H)^{r-2}\rangle=0.\]

Let us prove that (i) implies (ii). Applying the dévissage lemma (EGA III 3.1) and 4.5 (i), we reduce to the case where \(X\) is integral and \(F=\mathcal O_X\). Then we must prove that the polynomial \(\chi(L^{\otimes m})\) is constant. For this, we may replace \(L\) by a multiple, hence assume \(L\) algebraically equivalent to 0. In that case, if \(L\) is deformed to 0 by the algebraic family defined by an invertible sheaf \(M\) on some \(X\times_kT\) where \(T\) is connected, of finite type, the polynomial \(\chi(M_t^{\otimes m})\) does not depend on \(t\in T\) (EGA III 7.9.5); whence the implication.

Let \(Y\) be a closed integral curve in \(X\). Setting \(F=\mathcal O_Y\) in (ii), we find \(\chi(\mathcal O_Y)=\chi(L_Y)\). Hence the formula ("theorem of Riemann") \(\chi(L_Y)=\langle c_1(L).Y\rangle+\chi(\mathcal O_Y)\) gives \(\langle c_1(L).Y\rangle=0\); whence (ii) implies (iii).
Let \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) be a Chow presentation: \(X'\) is projective and \(f\) is surjective. If we grant that (iii) implies (i) for \(X'\), then
by virtue of 4.5, the implication for \(X\) follows. Suppose then from now on that \(X\) is projective, equipped with an ample sheaf \(H\).

Let \(X_i\) be the irreducible components of \(X\), equipped with the induced reduced structure. Applying 4.5 and EGA III 2.6.2 to the finite surjective morphism \(\coprod X_i\longrightarrow X\), we reduce the proof that (iii) implies (i) and (iv) to the case where \(X\) is integral.

If \(X\) is irreducible, it is evident that (ii) implies \(v\), that (iii) implies (vi), and, taking 2.1 into account, that \(v\) implies (vi), and that (vi) for \(X\) is equivalent to (vi) for \(X_{\mathrm{réd}}\) (X 4.4.1).

It therefore remains to see that (vi) implies (i) and (iv), and that (iv) implies (i), assuming \(X\) integral. But then, (vi) (resp. (iv)) implies that the family \(\mathfrak L\) of classes of the \(L^{\otimes m}\) (\(m\in\mathbb N\)) satisfies 3.13 (iii) (resp. (iv)), hence is bounded. There exists then an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal L\) on some \(X_T\), with \(T\) of finite type over \(k\), which bounds \(\mathfrak L\). Let \(p,q\) be two distinct integers such that \(L^{\otimes p}\) and \(L^{\otimes q}\) correspond to the same connected component of \(T\); then \(L^{\otimes(p-q)}\) is therefore algebraically equivalent to 0; whence (vi) (resp. (iv)) implies (i).

Finally, we see similarly that (vi) implies that there exists an integer \(N\) such that for every integer \(m\), \(L^{\otimes m}\otimes H^{\otimes N}\) is ample. Then for every \(m\), \((L^{\otimes m}\otimes H)^{\otimes N}\) is ample; whence (vi) implies (iv), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.7.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, and \(X\) a proper \(S\)-scheme. Then:

(i) The inclusion morphism \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) is representable by an open immersion.

(ii) If \(X\) is projective and integral over \(S\), then \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\)

is also representable by a closed immersion.

(iii) \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau}\) is of finite type over \(S\), that is, the family of classes of invertible sheaves \(\tau\)-equivalent to 0 on the fibres of \(X/S\) is bounded.

Indeed, in (i) (resp. (ii)), we must treat the case of a morphism \(T\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\), where \(T\) is an arbitrary \(S\)-scheme, and show that the inverse image of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau}\) is representable by an open (resp. and closed) subscheme of \(T\). It suffices to take \(T=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\) on an affine open subset \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) of \(S\). Then \(T\) is the limit of the filtered projective system of affine \(S\)-schemes \(T_\alpha=\operatorname{Spec}(B_\alpha)\), where \(B_\alpha\) runs through the sub-\(A\)-algebras of finite type of \(B\). By virtue of 3.1, the morphism in question comes from a morphism \(T_\alpha\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\). We may therefore assume \(T\) of finite type over \(S\).

The morphism \(T\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) corresponds to an f.p.p.f. morphism \(R\longrightarrow T\) and an invertible sheaf \(L\) on \(X_R\). By f.p.p.f. descent, we may replace \(T\) by \(R\). After the base change \(R\longrightarrow S\), taking into account the compatibility of the formation of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau}\) with any base change (4.1), we are thus reduced in (i) and (ii) to studying the case of a section \(S\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\), defined by an invertible sheaf \(L\) on \(X\).

We must prove that the set \(S_0\) of the \(s\in S\) such that \(L_s\) is \(\tau\)-equivalent to 0 is open, and also closed under the conditions of (ii); since \(S\) is noetherian, this means three things: for a \(s\in S_0\),
\(a\) every generization of \(s\) remains in \(S_0\).
\(b\) every specialization of \(s\), near \(s\), remains in \(S_0\).
\(c\) under the conditions of (ii), every specialization of \(s\) remains in \(S_0\).

Let \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) be a Chow presentation: \(X'\) is projective over \(S\); and \(f\) is surjective. If we grant \(a\), \(b\), \(c\) and (iii) for \(X'\), then they follow for \(X\) by virtue of 4.3 and 3.5. We may therefore assume \(X\) projective over \(S\).

Let \(H\) be ample for \(X/S\). If for some \(s\in S\), \((L^{\otimes m}\otimes H)_s\) is ample, then \((L^{\otimes m}\otimes H)_t\) is ample for every generization \(t\) of \(s\) (EGA III 4.7.1). Thanks to the equivalence of 4.6 (i) and (iv), we have thus established \(a\).

For \(b\), \(c\) and 4.7 (iii), we may assume \(S\) integral with generic point \(s\) in \(b\) and \(c\) (For \(b\) and \(c\), this is clear, and for (iii), it follows from 3.4 (i) applied to the morphism \(\coprod S_i\longrightarrow S\), where the \(S_i\) are the irreducible components of \(S\), equipped with the induced reduced structure).

There exist an integral scheme \(S'\) and an f.p.p.f. morphism \(S'\to S\), such that every irreducible component \(X_i'\) of \(X_{S'}\), equipped with the induced reduced structure, is integral over \(S'\) (XII 1.3). To prove \(b\) and (iii), it suffices to do so for \(X_{S'}/S'\), (in \(b\), by descent; in (iii), by noetherian induction and 3.4 (ii)).

Finally, consider the canonical surjective morphism \(\coprod X_i'\longrightarrow X_{S'}\). Then \(b\) and (iii) for \(X_{S'}/S'\) follow from \(c\) and (iii) for the \(X_i'/S'\), by virtue of 4.3, 3.5 and 3.7.

We are thus reduced to proving \(c\) and (iii), assuming \(S\) integral with generic point \(s\), and \(X\) projective and integral over \(S\). Let \(\mathcal O_X(1)\) be a very ample sheaf for \(X/S\). Then for every \(m,n\), \(\chi(L_t^{\otimes m}(n))\) and \(\chi(\mathcal O_{X_t}(n))\) do not depend on \(t\in S\) (EGA III 7.9.4). Hence \(c\) follows from 4.6 (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) \(v\), which also implies that the family of classes of invertible sheaves \(\tau\)-equivalent to 0 on the fibres of \(X/S\) has only one
single Hilbert polynomial; hence (iii) follows from 2.11.

Remark 4.8. Let \(X\) be proper and integral over the noetherian scheme \(S\). Without a projectivity hypothesis on \(X/S\), it is not known whether it remains true that \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) is a closed immersion, except when \(X\) is normal over \(S\) and \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) is representable (FGA 236, 2.3).
5. Finiteness theorems of "Néron-Severi" type.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1.

Let \(X\) be proper over the noetherian scheme \(S\); then the Néron-Severi groups \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X_K/K}(K)/\operatorname{Pic}_{X_K/K}^0(K)\) of the geometric fibres \(X_K/K\) of \(X/S\) are of finite type, and their rank and the order of their torsion subgroup \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X_K/K}^{\tau}(K)/\operatorname{Pic}_{X_K/K}^0(K)\) remain bounded above.

Indeed, by noetherian induction, we may restrict ourselves to proving 5.1 with \(S\) replaced by an arbitrary non-empty open subset of \(S_{\mathrm{réd}}\); thus we may assume \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau}\) representable by a \(S\)-scheme of finite type (XII 1.2 and 4.7 (iii)). Then the order of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X_K/K}^{\tau}(K)/\operatorname{Pic}_{X_K/K}^0(K)\) is equal to the number of connected components of \((\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^{\tau})_K\), a number which remains the same over a non-empty open subset of \(S\) (EGA IV 9.7.9).

It remains to bound the rank of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X_K/K}(K)/\operatorname{Pic}_{X_K/K}^{\tau}(K)\). As in the proof of 4.7 (iii), we easily reduce to the case \(S\) integral, and \(X\) projective and integral over \(S\). Let \(r\) be the common dimension of the fibres of \(X/S\). If \(r\) is 0 (resp. 1), then this rank is evidently 0 (resp. 1).
If \(r\) is \(\geq2\), we shall reduce to the case \(r=2\) and \(X\) smooth over \(S\). After possibly shrinking \(S\) we can find a linear section \(Y\) of \(X\) which is

still integral over \(S\), with fibres of dimension 2; by virtue of 4.3, we may replace \(X\) by \(Y\). Finally, by virtue of 4.3 and 3.10 (using resolution of singularities of surfaces), after possibly replacing \(S\) by a flat covering of a non-empty open subset of \(S\), we may replace \(X\) by a smooth \(S\)-scheme.
Now, thanks to 4.6 (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iii) and to the theorem of finiteness and specialization of étale cohomology groups (SGA 4 XVI 2.2), 5.1 follows from the following proposition.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(X\) a proper and smooth \(k\)-scheme. Then the group \(C_{\mathrm{num}}^p(X)\) of algebraic cycles of codimension \(p\) on \(X\), modulo numerical equivalence, is free of rank \(\leq b^{2p}\), the Betti number.

Indeed, if one forgets the action of the roots of 1, the étale cohomology \(H^*(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)=_{\mathrm{dfn}}[\varprojlim H^*(X,\mathbb Z/\ell^\nu\mathbb Z)]\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\), where \((\ell,\operatorname{car}(k))=1\), becomes a Weil cohomology: it satisfies Poincaré duality and the Kunneth formula; and it receives a functorial, non-zero homomorphism from the ring of algebraic cycles modulo rational equivalence \(C_{\mathrm{rat}}^*(X)\) (see X 7.13).

Let \(a_1,\ldots,a_m\in C_{\mathrm{rat}}^{r-p}(X)\), where \(r=\dim(X)\), be elements which map onto a basis of the \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-vector space in \(H^{2(r-p)}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\), generated by the algebraic cycles. Consider the homomorphism
\[\alpha:C_{\mathrm{rat}}^p(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^m\]

defined by \(\alpha(b)=(\langle b.a_1\rangle,\ldots,\langle b.a_m\rangle)\), where \(\langle\ \rangle\) denotes the intersection number. Then one sees easily that the image of \(\alpha\) is \(C_{\mathrm{num}}^p(X)\).

Remark 5.3. Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, and \(X\) a proper and smooth surface over \(k\). Then the second Betti number \(b^2(X)\) can be interpreted as being \(c_2(X)-2+2b^1(X)\), where \(b^1(X)=2\dim(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k})\), and \(c_2(X)=\) the second Chern class \(=\) the Euler-Poincaré characteristic \(=\) the intersection number of the diagonal of \(X\times X\) with itself; thus \(b^2(X)\) has an interpretation independent of cohomology. The inequality \(\operatorname{rang}(C_{\mathrm{num}}^1(X))\leq b^2(X)\) in this form for \(\operatorname{car}(k)>0\) is due (before étale cohomology) to IGUSA, who used deep results on the structure of the tame fundamental group of an algebraic curve minus a few points.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.4.

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, and \(X\) a proper \(k\)-scheme. Then there exist a finite number of closed integral curves \(Y_1,\ldots,Y_\rho\) in \(X\), with the following property: for a subset \(\Lambda\) of the scheme \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}\) to be quasi-compact, it is necessary and sufficient that the intersection numbers \(\langle\lambda.Y_i\rangle\) of the \(\lambda\in\Lambda\) remain bounded. Moreover, one can take for \(\rho\) the rank of the Néron-Severi group \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}(k)/\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}^0(k)\).

Indeed, since \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}^{\tau}\) is a quasi-compact open subgroup of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}\) (4.7), for \(\Lambda\) to be quasi-compact it is necessary and sufficient that the corresponding set of points of the vector space \([\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}(k)/\operatorname{Pic}_{X/k}^{\tau}(k)]\otimes\mathbb Q\) be finite. But \(\tau\)-equivalence is equal to numerical equivalence (4.6 (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iii)), so we can choose among the curves in \(X\) curves which map onto a basis of the dual space; whence the assertion.
6. Appendix: Study of \(b\)-sheaves on \(P=\mathbb P_k^N\).
6.1. Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, and \(P=\mathbb P_k^N\). Let \(F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(P\), with Hilbert polynomial
\[\chi(F(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i{n+i\choose i},\]
and
\[(c)=(c_0,\ldots,c_r)\]
a sequence of integers \(\geq(a)\). Let
\[a_{m,i}=\sum_{j=0}^{r-i}a_{j+i}{m-1+j\choose j}\quad\text{et}\quad c_{m,i}=\sum_{j=0}^{r-i}c_{j+i}{m-1+j\choose j}.\]
Finally, for every integer \(q\geq1\), denote the largest coherent subsheaf of \(F\) with support of dimension \(\leq q-1\) by \(N_q\), and let \(F_q=F/N_q\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2.

Under the conditions of 6.1, consider an exact sequence

\[0\longrightarrow I\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P^{\oplus M}\longrightarrow F(m)\longrightarrow0\]

with \(m\geq0\). Then \(I\) is \(p\)-regular with \(p=P_r(c_{m,0},\ldots,c_{m,r})\), where \(P_r\) is the \(r\)-th \(b\)-polynomial (1.9).
Indeed, \(\mathcal O_P\) is evidently a \((0,\ldots,0,1)\)-sheaf (\(N+1\) terms), so \(I\) is a \((0,\ldots,0,M)\)-sheaf by 1.6. Thanks to 2.10,
\[\chi(I(n))=M{n+N\choose N}-\sum_{i=0}^r a_{m,i}{n+i\choose i}.\]
Hence the assertion follows from 1.11 and 1.10.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.3.

Under the conditions of 6.1, we assume \(F\) is a \(b\)-sheaf. Then one can compute, by means of universal polynomials in the \(a_i\), \(b_i\) and \(N\), integers \(m\), \(M\), \(m_1\) and \(M_1\) such that one has an exact sequence

\[\mathcal O_P(-m_1)^{\oplus M_1}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P(-m)^{\oplus M}\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow0.\]

Moreover, \(N\) appears only in the computation of \(M_1\).
Indeed, we take \(m=P_{r-1}(c_0,\ldots,c_{r-1})\) with \(c_i=b_i-a_i\), and
\[M=\sum a_i{m+i\choose i},\]
so that we have an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow I\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P^{\oplus M}\longrightarrow F(m)\longrightarrow0\]
by virtue of 1.11 and 1.3. Similarly, taking 6.2 into account, we take \(m_1=m+p\) with \(p=P_r(a_{m,0},\ldots,a_{m,r})\), and \(M_1=M{p+N\choose N}-\sum a_i{m_1+i\choose i}\) (= \(\chi(I(p))\)).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.4.

Under the conditions of 6.1, we assume \(F\) is a quotient of \(\mathcal O_P(-m)^{\oplus M}\) with \(m\geq0\). Let \(b_i=P_{r-i}(c_{m,i},\ldots,c_{m,r})\) for \(i=0,\ldots,r\), where \(P_s\) is the \(s\)-th \(b\)-polynomial (1.9); let \(b=b_1+m-1\), and let

\[B=\sum_{i=1}^r a_i{b+i\choose i}.\]

Then:

(i) \(F\) is \(b\)-regular.

(ii) \(-B\leq a_0=h^0(F(b))-B\).

(iii) \(F\) is a \((b_0,\ldots,b_r)\)-sheaf.
Indeed, let us take a section \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal O_P(1)\) such that we obtain a commutative diagram with exact rows and columns

\[\begin{array}{ccccccccc} &0&&0&&0\\ &\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&I(-1)&\longrightarrow&I&\longrightarrow&J&\longrightarrow&0\\ &\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&\mathcal O_P(-1)^{\oplus M}&\longrightarrow&\mathcal O_P^{\oplus M}&\longrightarrow&\mathcal O_Y^{\oplus M}&\longrightarrow&0\\ &\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&F(m-1)&\longrightarrow&F(m)&\longrightarrow&G(m)&\longrightarrow&0\\ &\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ &0&&0&&0 \end{array}\]
Since \(\chi(G(n))=\sum_{i=0}^{r-1}a_{i+1}{n+i\choose i}\) by 1.7, \(J\) is \(b_1\)-regular by 6.2; hence \(H^q(I(p))=0\) for \(q\geq2\) and \(p\geq b_1-q\) by 1.4 (i). The exact sequence
\[H^q(\mathcal O_P^{\oplus M}(p))\longrightarrow H^q(F(m+p))\longrightarrow H^{q+1}(I(p))\]
then shows that \(F(m)\) is \((b_1-1)\)-regular; whence (i).
Assertion (i) implies that \(0\leq h^0(F(b))=\chi(F(b))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i{b+i\choose i}\); whence (ii). By 2.10 and 1.9,
\[\chi(F(b))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_{m,i}{b_1-1+i\choose i}\leq b_1+\sum_{i=0}^r c_{m,i}{b_1-1+i\choose i}=b_0\ ;\]
hence, \(h^0(F(-1))\leq h^0(F(b))\leq b_0\). By induction on \(r\), we may assume that \(G\) is a \((b_1,\ldots,b_r)\)-sheaf; whence (iii) thanks to 1.6.2.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.5.

Under the conditions 6.1, we assume \(F\) without associated prime cycle of dimension 0.

(i) If \(h^0(F)\geq1\), then \(h^0(F(-1))\leq h^0(F)-1\).

(ii) \(H^0(F(-n))=0\) for \(n\geq h^0(F)\).

(iii) Let there be an exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow F(-1)\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow0\) and an integer \(n_0\geq0\) such that \(H^0(G(-n_0))=0\); then \(H^0(F(-n_0))=0\) and \(h^0(F)\leq n_0h^0(G)\).

Indeed, let \(\sigma\in H^0(F)\), \(\sigma\neq0\); then \(\sigma\) defines a non-zero subsheaf \(F'=\mathcal O_X.\sigma\) of \(F\), so \(s=\dim.\operatorname{supp}(F')\) is \(\geq1\). Take a commutative diagram with exact rows and columns

\[\begin{array}{ccccccccc} &0&&0&&0\\&\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&F'(-1)&\longrightarrow&F'&\longrightarrow&G'&\longrightarrow&0\\ &\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&F(-1)&\longrightarrow&F&\longrightarrow&G&\longrightarrow&0 \end{array}\]

From \(s\geq1\), it follows that \(G'\neq0\); consequently, \(F'\) is not contained in \(F(-1)\); that is, the section \(\sigma\) is not contained in the image of the injection \(H^0(F(-1))\longrightarrow H^0(F)\), whence (i).
Assertion (ii) follows immediately from (i), taking into account that \(h^0(F)=0\) evidently implies \(h^0(F(-1))=0\), since \(h^0(F(-1))\leq h^0(F)\).

In (iii), we find \(0\leq h^0(F(n))-h^0(F(n-1))\leq h^0(G(n))\). Hence for \(n\geq n_0\), \(h^0(F(-n_0))=h^0(F(-n))\) since \(h^0(G(-n_0))=0\); hence \(h^0(F(-n_0))=0\) by (ii). Consequently, \(h^0(F(p-n_0))\leq h^0(G(1-n_0))+\cdots+h^0(G(p-n_0))\leq ph^0(G(p-n_0))\) for \(p\geq1\); whence (iii).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.6.

Under the conditions of 6.1, we assume \(F\) is a \((b_0,\ldots,b_r)\)-sheaf. Then \(F_q\) is a \(((b_{q-1})^q,\ldots,(b_{q-1})^2,b_{q-1},b_q,\ldots,b_r)\)-sheaf.
Indeed, let \(\sigma\) be a "general" section of \(\mathcal O_X(1)\); then \(\sigma\) defines a commutative diagram with exact rows and columns

\[\begin{array}{ccccccccc} &0&&0&&0\\&\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&N_q(-1)&\longrightarrow&N_q&\longrightarrow&G'&\longrightarrow&0\\ &\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&F(-1)&\longrightarrow&F&\longrightarrow&G&\longrightarrow&0\\ &\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\ 0&\longrightarrow&F_q(-1)&\longrightarrow&F_q&\longrightarrow&G''&\longrightarrow&0\\ &\downarrow&&\downarrow&&\downarrow\\&0&&0&&0 \end{array}\]
where \(G\) is a \((b_1,\ldots,b_r)\)-sheaf (1.6 (ii)) and where \(G'=G_{q-1}\) (a question of depth).

Suppose \(q=1\). Then \(\dim.\operatorname{supp}(N_q)=0\); hence \(H^1(N_q(-1))=0\) and \(h^0(F_q(-1))\leq h^0(F(-1))\leq b_0\). Moreover, \(G'=0\); hence \(G\simeq G''\). Thus, \(F_1\) is indeed a \((b_0,\ldots,b_r)\)-sheaf. Furthermore, 6.5 (ii) shows that \(H^0(F_1(-b_0))=0\). If \(q\geq2\), we may assume \(G_{q-1}\) is a \(((b_{q-1})^{q-1},\ldots,b_{q-1},\ldots,b_r)\)-sheaf and \(H^0(G_{q-1}(-b_{q-1}))=0\), arguing by induction on \(q\). Then 6.5 shows that \(H^0(F_q(-b_{q-1}))=0\) and \(h^0(F_q(-1))\leq(b_{q-1})^q\); whence the assertion.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.7.

There exist two sequences \(\{A_i(X_0,\ldots,X_i\ ;Y)\}\) and \(\{A_i^{(q)}(X_0,\ldots,X_q\ ;Y)\}\) of polynomials with the following properties. We assume the conditions of 6.1 satisfied and \(F\) a quotient of \(\mathcal O_P(-m)^{\oplus M}\) with \(m\geq0\).

(i) If \(F\) is a \((b_0,\ldots,b_r)\)-sheaf, then for \(i=0,\ldots,r\),

\[|a_i|\leq A_{r-i}(b_i,\ldots,b_r\ ;m).\]

(ii) If \(\chi(F_q(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i^{(q)}{n+i\choose i}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of \(F_q\), then for \(i=0,\ldots,q-1\),

\[|a_i^{(q)}|\leq A_{r-i}^{(r-q)}(c_{q-1},\ldots,c_r\ ;m).\]

(Note that \(a_{q-1}^{(q)}\leq a_{q-1}\) and \(a_q^{(q)}=a_q,\ldots,a_r^{(q)}=a_r\) since \(\dim.\operatorname{supp}(N_q)\leq q-1\)).

Indeed, let there be an exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow F(-1)\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow0\) such that \(G\) is a \((b_1,\ldots,b_r)\)-sheaf; arguing by induction on \(r\), we may assume \(A_0,\ldots,A_{r-1}\) defined, taking 1.7 into account. Then 6.4 (ii) exhibits the definition of \(A_r\), taking 1.8 into account. Finally (ii) follows formally from 6.4 (iii), 6.6, and assertion (i) (where we take \(F_q\) for \(F\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.8. (Grothendieck).

Let \(X\) be projective over the noetherian scheme \(S\), \(\mathcal O_X(1)\) very ample for \(X/S\), and \(\mathcal F\) a family of classes of coherent sheaves on the fibres of \(X/S\). We assume:

\(a\) There exists a coherent sheaf \(E\) on \(X\) such that \(\mathcal F\) is contained in the family of classes of quotients of sheaves of the form \(E_K\).

(bq) In the Hilbert polynomials \(\chi(F_K(n))\) of the \(F_K\in\mathcal F\), the coefficients in degrees \(\geq q-1\) remain bounded above.

Then the \((F_K)_q\) (when \(F_K\) remains in \(\mathcal F\)) form a bounded family; moreover the coefficients in \(\chi(F_K(n))\) in degrees \(\geq q-2\) remain bounded below.

Indeed, we may evidently assume \(X=\mathbb P_S^N\) and \(E=\mathcal O_X(-m)^{\oplus M}\). Then the first assertion follows formally from 6.7 (ii) and 1.13; the second follows, by arguing by induction with the aid of an exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow F(-1)\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow0\), from 6.4 (ii).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.9.

Let \(k\) be a field. We call a special positive \(k\)-cycle of dimension \(r\) any projective \(k\)-scheme \(X\), equipped with a very ample sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(1)\), which is a union of closed subschemes \(X_j\) of dimension \(r\), \(X_j\) being obtained by a base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(k_j)\) as the inverse image of an integral \(k_j\)-scheme \(X_j^1\) (equipped with \(\mathcal O_{X_j}(1)\)). We call the degree of \(X\) the coefficient \(a_r\) in the Hilbert polynomial \(\chi(\mathcal O_X(n))=\sum a_i{n+i\choose i}\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.10.

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(X\) a special positive \(k\)-cycle of dimension \(r\) and degree \(d\). Then \(\mathcal O_X\) is a \((0,\ldots,0,d)\)-sheaf.

Indeed, let \(X\) be in \(\mathbb P_k^N\). After possibly replacing \(k\) by the algebraic closure of a purely transcendental extension, which is permissible thanks to 1.6 (i), we may assume that there exist \(r(N+1)\) elements in \(k\) which are algebraically independent over all the \(k_j\). Then the \(r\) successive "generic" hyperplane sections of \(X\), defined by means of these elements, are still special positive \(k\)-cycles of degree \(d\).

Arguing by induction on \(r\), it thus remains to note that when \(r=0\), then \(h^0(\mathcal O_X(-1))=d\), and when \(r\geq1\), then \(h^0(\mathcal O_X(-1))=0\). For this last point, we may assume that \(X\) itself comes from an integral scheme \(X'\), thanks to the injection \(\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow\prod_j\mathcal O_{X_j}\); but then \(h^0(\mathcal O_X(-1))=h^0(\mathcal O_{X'}(-1))\), which is 0 since \(H^0(\mathcal O_{X'})\) is a field.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.11.

(i) Let \(r,d\) be two integers, \(c_i=A_{r-i}(0,\ldots,0,d;0)\) for \(i=0,\ldots,r\) (6.7 (i)), and \(p=P_r(c_0,\ldots,c_r)\) (1.9). Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, and \(X\) a special positive \(k\)-cycle of dimension \(\leq r\) and degree \(\leq d\), with Hilbert polynomial \(\chi(\mathcal O_X(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i{n+i\choose i}\). Then for \(i=0,\ldots,r\), we have

\[|a_i|\leq c_i.\]

Moreover, \(X\) embeds as a subscheme of \(\mathbb P_k^N\) with \(N=d(r+1)-1\), defined by (at most) \({N+p\choose N}\) equations of degree \(p\).

(ii) (Chow) Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme. When \(K\) runs through the algebraically closed extensions of the \(k(s)\), \(s\in S\), the special positive \(K\)-cycles of bounded dimension and degree form a "bounded family" (either with the \(X\) considered as subschemes of a fixed \(\mathbb P_S^N\), or in an evident abstract sense).

Indeed, in (i), the first assertion follows from 6.7 (i); the second, from 6.10 and 1.8 (i), and from 6.2 (applied to \(0\longrightarrow I\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow0\), \(P=\mathbb P_k^N\)) and 1.3. Finally, (ii) follows from (i) and 1.13.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.12.

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, and \(X\) a projective \(k\)-scheme purely of dimension \(r\), without embedded associated prime cycle. If one allows nilpotent elements in \(\mathcal O_X\) in an arbitrary way, one can no longer bound the coefficients in the Hilbert polynomial \(\chi(\mathcal O_X(n))=\sum_{i=0}^r a_i{n+i\choose i}\) in terms of \(a_r\) alone.
