For example, let \(Z\) be a smooth curve of degree \(d\) in \(\mathbb P_k^3\). For every \(n\gg0\), there exists a smooth surface \(Y\) of degree \(n\) which contains \(Z\). Then \(Z\) is a Cartier divisor on \(Y\); let \(\mathcal O_{X_n}=\mathcal O_Y/\mathcal O_Y(-2Z)\). Finally, we have the exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X(-Z)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow\mathcal O_Z\longrightarrow0\) and the formula \(\langle Z^2\rangle_Y=2p_a(Z)-2-(n-4)d\); whence \(a_0=\chi(\mathcal O_{X_n}(-1))\longrightarrow\infty\) (\(n\longrightarrow\infty\)), although \(a_1=2d\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.13.

Under the conditions of 6.1, consider an exact sequence

\[\mathcal O_P(-m_1)^{\oplus M_1}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_P(-m)^{\oplus M}\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\alpha}F\longrightarrow0\]

with \(m_1\geq m\geq0\). Then for \(i=0,\ldots,N\),

\[|a_i|\leq A_{N-i}(0,\ldots,0,M;m_1)+M{m\choose N-i}.\]

Indeed, let \(I=\operatorname{Ker}(\alpha)\); evidently, \(I\) is a \((0,\ldots,0,M)\)-sheaf, and \(\chi(I(n))=\sum_{i=0}^N[M{-m-1+N-i\choose N-i}-a_i]{n+i\choose i}\); whence the assertion thanks to 6.7 (i), and the formula \({-m-1+Q\choose Q}=(-1)^Q{m\choose Q}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.14. (I. Hermann).

For every \(N\geq0\), there exists a polynomial \(R_N(m)\) such that for every field \(k_0\) and every ideal of the polynomial ring \(k_0[T_1,\ldots,T_N]\) generated by elements of degree \(\leq m\), the radical \(\sqrt I\) of \(I\) is generated by elements of degree \(\leq R_N(m)\).

Indeed, let \(k\) be the algebraic closure of \(k_0\). Introducing an auxiliary indeterminate, consider \(Y\) (resp. \(X\)) the subscheme of \(P=\mathbb P_k^N\) defined by the homogenized ideal of \(I\) (resp. of \(\sqrt I\)). In the Hilbert polynomial \(\chi(\mathcal O_Y(n))=\sum_{i=0}^N b_i{n+i\choose i}\), we have \(|b_i|\leq c_i\) with \(c_i=A_{N-i}(0,\ldots,0,1;m)\) by 6.13.

Let \(X=\bigcup X^q\) be the decomposition according to dimension. Then degree \((X^q)\leq e_q\) with \(e_q=P_{N-q}(c_q,\ldots,c_N)\). Indeed, after possibly cutting \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) by a "general" linear space of codimension \(q\), we may assume \(q=0\). Then evidently, degree \((X^q)=h^0(\mathcal O_{X^q})\) and \(h^0(\mathcal O_{X^q})\leq h^0(\mathcal O_Y)\), and by virtue of 6.4 (iii), \(h^0(\mathcal O_Y)\leq e_q\).

Consequently, \(\mathcal O_{X^q}\) is a \((0,\ldots,0,e_q)\)-sheaf thanks to 6.10. It therefore follows from the injection \(\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow\prod\mathcal O_{X^q}\) that \(\mathcal O_X\) is a \((e_0,\ldots,e_N)\)-sheaf. Consequently, in the Hilbert polynomial \(\chi(\mathcal O_X(n))=\sum_{i=0}^N a_i{n+i\choose i}\), we have \(|a_i|\leq f_i\), with \(f_i=A_{N-i}(e_i,\ldots,e_N;0)\) by 6.7. Finally the ideal \(\sqrt I\) of \(X\) is \(R_N(m)\)-regular with \(R_N(m)=P_N(f_0,\ldots,f_N)\); whence the assertion thanks to 1.3.
7. Appendix: Hodge index theorem
Here we recover and complete some earlier results by a direct argument.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.1.

Let \(X\) be an irreducible surface, proper over an algebraically closed field. We assume that there exists an invertible sheaf \(H\) on \(X\) such that \(\langle c_1(H)^2\rangle>0\) (for example, \(H\) ample). Then an invertible sheaf \(L\) on \(X\) such that

\[\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}c_1(H)\rangle=0\quad\text{et}\quad\langle c_1(L)^2\rangle\geq0,\]

is numerically equivalent to 0. (Consequently, the intersection form on \(A^1_{\mathrm{num}}(X)_{\mathbb Q}\) \((=[\operatorname{Pic}(X)/\operatorname{Pic}^\tau(X)]\otimes\mathbb Q\), by 4.6) is non-degenerate of type \((1,\rho-1)\)).

7.1.1. First suppose: \(X\) reduced (hence, integral); \(H\) ample; \(\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}c_1(H)\rangle=0\); and \(\langle c_1(L)^2\rangle>0\). Arguing by contradiction, take \(m>0\) such that \(H_1=L\otimes H^{\otimes m}\) is ample, and take \(n\) such that, setting \(N=L^{\otimes n}\otimes H_1^{-1}\), we have that \(\langle c_1(N)\mathbin{.}c_1(H_1)\rangle=n\langle c_1(L)^2\rangle-m\langle c_1(H)^2\rangle\) is \(>0\). Then we also have that \(\langle c_1(N)\mathbin{.}c_1(H)\rangle=-m\langle c_1(H)^2\rangle\) is \(<0\), and that \(\langle c_1(N)^2\rangle=n^2\langle c_1(L)^2\rangle+\langle c_1(H)^2\rangle\) is \(>0\), which contradicts the following lemma.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.1.2.

Let \(X\) be an integral surface, projective over an algebraically closed field. For an invertible sheaf \(N\) on \(X\) such that \(\langle c_1(N)^2\rangle>0\), the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) For every ample sheaf \(H\), we have \(\langle c_1(N)\mathbin{.}c_1(H)\rangle>0\).

\(i'\) There exists an ample sheaf \(H\) such that we have \(\langle c_1(N)\mathbin{.}c_1(H)\rangle>0\).

(ii) There exists an integer \(n>0\) such that we have \(H^0(X,N^{\otimes n})\neq0\).
Indeed, (ii) implies that there exists an effective divisor \(D\) such that \(N^{\otimes n}\simeq\mathcal O_X(D)\), and \(\langle c_1(N)^2\rangle>0\) implies that \(D>0\); whence (i).

To see that \(i'\) implies (ii), we may evidently assume that \(H=\mathcal O_X(1)\) is very ample; then there exists a closed integral, locally principal curve \(Y\) such that \(H=\mathcal O_X(Y)\). By the theorem of Riemann, \(i'\) implies that \(H^1(N_Y^{\otimes n}(p))=0\) for \(p\geq0\) and \(n\geq n_0\), with \(n_0=2h^1(\mathcal O_Y)-1\). Since \(H^2(N^{\otimes n}(p))=0\) for \(p\gg0\), the exact sequence
\[H^1(N_Y^{\otimes n}(p))\longrightarrow H^2(N^{\otimes n}(p-1))\longrightarrow H^2(N^{\otimes n}(p))\longrightarrow0\]
gives that \(H^2(N^{\otimes n})=0\) for \(n\geq n_0\). Hence for \(n\gg0\), we have
\[h^0(N^{\otimes n})=\chi(N^{\otimes n})+h^1(N^{\otimes n})\geq\chi(N^{\otimes n})=\tfrac12\langle c_1(N)^2\rangle n^2+\cdots>0,\]
which completes the proof of the lemma.

7.1.3. Now suppose in 7.1 that there exists an invertible sheaf \(M\) on \(X\) such that \(\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}c_1(M) angle eq0\), and let us argue by contradiction. Let us replace \(X\) by the underlying reduced scheme of a Chow presentation of \(X\), which is permissible thanks to the projection formula X 4.4.4, and let \(G\) be ample on \(X\). Take \(m>0\) such that \(H_1=G^{\otimes m}\otimes M\) is ample, and such that \(\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}c_1(H_1) angle eq0\), take \(p,q\) (\(q eq0\)) such that, setting \(L_1=L^{\otimes p}\otimes H_1^{\otimes q}\), we have that \(\langle c_1(L_1)\mathbin{.}c_1(H_1) angle=p\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}c_1(H_1) angle+q\langle c_1(H_1)\mathbin{.}c_1(H_1) angle\) is 0; then we also have that \(\langle c_1(L_1)^2 angle=p^2\langle c_1(L)^2 angle+q^2\langle c_1(H_1)^2 angle\) is \(>0\); which brings us back to the case 7.1.1, with \(L=L_1\) and \(H=H_1\).

7.1.4. Finally let us consider the general case of 7.1. Still arguing by contradiction, suppose that \(L\) is not numerically equivalent to 0; moreover, let us replace \(X\) by the normalization of \(X_{\mathrm{réd}}\), so that \(X\) has

only a finite number of singular points, a reduction justified by the projection formula X 4.4.4 and by 4.5 (ii). There exists then a closed integral curve \(Y\) on \(X\) such that \(\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}Y\rangle\neq0\), and \(Y\) is locally principal outside a finite set \(Z\). Let \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) be the blow-up of the ideal \(I\) of \(\mathcal O_Y\). The ideal \(M=I.\mathcal O_{X'}\) is invertible, and it defines a subscheme of \(X'\), which is the union of two subschemes \(Y'\), \(Y''\) such that \(f(Y')=Y\) and \(f(Y'')\subset Z\). Thanks to the projection formula X 4.4.4, we have that \(\langle c_1(f^*L)\mathbin{.}c_1(M)\rangle=-\langle c_1(f^*L)\mathbin{.}Y'\rangle-\langle c_1(f^*L)\mathbin{.}Y''\rangle\) is equal to \(-\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}Y\rangle-0\neq0\), and we may replace \(X,L,H\) by \(X',f^*L,f^*H\); we are thus reduced to the preceding case, and the proof of 7.1 is complete.

Remark 7.2. It is not known whether an irreducible, proper surface (over an algebraically closed field), which possesses an invertible sheaf \(H\) such that \(\langle c_1(H)^2\rangle>0\), is necessarily projective. It is likewise not known whether for every irreducible, proper surface, the intersection form on \(A^1_{\mathrm{num}}(X)_\mathbb Q\) is non-degenerate; it follows from 7.1 that when it is degenerate, it is necessarily negative (non-definite).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.3.

Under the conditions of 7.1, let \(M\) be an invertible sheaf on \(X\) which is not numerically equivalent to \(H\), and let

\[d=\langle c_1(M)\mathbin{.}c_1(H)\rangle\quad\text{et}\quad h=\langle c_1(H)^2\rangle.\]

Then, \(\langle c_1(M)^2\rangle<d^2/h\).
Indeed, let \(L=M^{\otimes h}\otimes H^{\otimes-d}\). Since \(\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}c_1(H)\rangle=0\), by 7.1, \(\langle c_1(L)^2\rangle<0\); whence the assertion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.4.

Let \(X\) be an irreducible scheme of dimension \(r\geq2\), which is projective over an algebraically closed field, and let \(H\) be an ample sheaf on \(X\). Then:

(i) For an invertible sheaf \(L\) on \(X\) to be numerically equivalent to 0, it is (necessary and) sufficient that

\[\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}c_1(H)^{r-1}\rangle=0\quad\text{et}\quad\langle c_1(L)^2\mathbin{.}c_1(H)^{r-2}\rangle\geq0.\]

(ii) For every invertible sheaf \(M\) on \(X\), which is not numerically equivalent to \(H\), we have

\[\langle c_1(M)^2\mathbin{.}c_1(H)^{r-2}\rangle<\langle c_1(M)\mathbin{.}c_1(H)^{r-1}\rangle^2/\langle c_1(H)^r\rangle.\]

Indeed, (ii) follows at once from (i), by the argument of 7.3. To establish (i), let \(Y\) be a closed, integral curve in \(X\); we want to prove that \(\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}Y angle=0\). Let us embed \(X\) in \(\mathbb P_k^N\) by means of \(H^{\otimes n}\) (\(n>0\)). Let \(I= igoplus I_ u\) be the homogeneous ideal of \(Y\), and \( u_0\) an integer such that \(I_{ u_0}\) generates \(I_ u\) for \( u\geq u_0\). Then the hypersurfaces \(Z\) of degree \( u_0\) which contain \(Y\) have no common points outside \(Y\). Arguing by induction on \(r=\dim(X)\), one finds with the aid of Bertini's theorem \(r-2\) hypersurfaces \(Z_1,\ldots,Z_{r-2}\) of degree \( u_0\) which contain \(Y\), such that \(X'=Z_1\cap\cdots\cap Z_{r-2}\cap X\) is irreducible. Since \(\langle c_1(L)\mathbin{.}Y angle=\langle c_1(L_{X'})\mathbin{.}Y angle\), it suffices to prove that \(L_{X'}\) is numerically equivalent to 0 on \(X'\). Now by the projection formula, we have that \(\langle c_1(L_{X'})\mathbin{.}c_1(H_{X'}) angle=0\) and \(\langle c_1(L_{X'})^2 angle\geq0\); hence the assertion follows from 7.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.5.

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(Y\) a projective \(k\)-scheme, equipped with an ample sheaf \(H\), and \(X\) the scheme of zeros of a section \(\varphi\) of \(H\). We assume that for every irreducible component \(Y^i\) of \(Y\),
the irreducible components of \(X\cap Y^i=V(\varphi|Y^i)\) are of dimension \(\geq2\). Then the morphism \(A^1_{\mathrm{num}}(Y)\longrightarrow A^1_{\mathrm{num}}(X)\) is injective.
Indeed, since we may evidently assume \(Y\) integral, the assertion follows from 7.4 (i), thanks to the projection formula.
